issue_title: File sizes/resources for granular import/exports are overestimated
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

1. In Export mode, selecting a topic will say _every_ resource under topic is exported
1. In RemoteImport mode, selecting a topic will say _every_ resource under topic is imported
1. In Local Import mode, selecting a topic will say _every_ resource under topic is imported

And the file sizes reflect those high resource counts
### Expected behavior

1. In Export mode, selecting a topic will say only resources that are _on the exporting device_ are exported
1. In RemoteImport mode, selecting a topic will say resources _not yet on the importing device_ are imported
1. In Local Import mode, selecting a topic will say resources _not yet on the importing device & on the importing device_ are imported. (Due to technical limitations, we do not know how many Resources are on the importing device, for an arbitrary topic).

### User-facing consequences

1. Incorrect estimates
1. User being blocked from completing transfer if system thinks transfer goes over capacity

issue_title: Support for client certificates
issue_body:
We utilize a MISP instance where MISP is protected by SSL/TLS and requires client certificate auth, i.e.

```
<Directory /var/www/MISP/app/webroot>
    SSLVerifyClient require
    ...
```

If I'm not mistaken, it is the case that a remote client using PyMISP cannot currently interact with this MISP REST API. Would it be possible to add support for supplying client certificate data in PyMISP to interact with such deployments?

Noteworthy that this seems fairly simplistic using Requests: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification. 

In our use case we'd have various PyMISP clients configured with a user settings file and would add such parameters (key and certificate files, or simply combined key+cert file) in there.

issue_title: charmsupport nrpe
issue_body:
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.2/charmhelpers/contrib/charmsupport/nrpe.py#L164-L177

What is the purpose of this function? This is just weird. 

what this does, is to force the user to add all files to one of the directories in search_path. 
If the file does not exist, check_cmd will be empty, nrpe will restart and break nrpe on this unit. 

you cannot for example use 'sudo /usr/lib/nagios/plugins/check_something' as check_cmd, since sudo is not in the correct path. a workaround for this, is to create a wrapper which only function is to call 'sudo /usr/lib/nagios/plugins/check_something'

if one still need to micromanage the user, maybe extend the search_path to os.environ['PATH']
and if the command is not found, dont create the file with an empty check_cmd, which breaks nrpe. and be sure to log this action

issue_title: Better stopping criteria
issue_body:
As far as I understand, the current stopping criterion used in pyamg is `norm(r_k) / norm(r_0) < eps`, where `eps` is specified with the `tol` parameter of the multigrid solver.

I understand that setting a fixed tolerance this way is useful to assess the efficiency of the solver regarding how fast the residue is reduced, but it does not provide any indication of how good the resulting approximation actually is. Obviously, if `norm(r_0)` is big, then also `norm(r_k)` will be big for a fixed `eps`. In practice, using `norm(r_k) / (norm(A) norm(x_k) + norm(b))` or at least `norm(r_k) / norm(b)` is much better. In some cases estimates involving `norm(x_k - x_{k-1})` might be useful as well.

For pyamg, it would be nice if the user did not have to write `tol=eps * norm(b) / norm(b - A*x0)` or something like that in order to enforce custom criterion. This trick is still insufficient for the last case.

issue_title: Immediately bring down a BGP peer upon failure of the underlying TCP session

In situations where fast convergence is paramount, this can greatly speed up the overall convergence time (especially if ExaBGP already knows backup paths).

issue_title: Legacy config file format
issue_body:
Hey there,

I recently noticed a bad/wrong behaviour of this library when using `docker-compose pull`.

docker-compose.yml:

```
version: '2'
services:

  test:
    image: eu.gcr.io/some/private/image:latest
```

If I authenticate myself using `gcloud docker login && gcloud docker -a`, I cannot pull the image with docker-compose. When I log in with `docker login -u _token -p $(gcloud auth print-access-token) eu.gcr.io`, it works.

Furthermore, I can pull the exact same image doing `docker pull eu.gcr.io/some/private/image:latest` after logging in with `gcloud docker login && gcloud docker -a`. (This is why I post here instead of creating an issue in [google-cloud-sdk](https://github.com/google-cloud-sdk/google-cloud-sdk))

I looked at the source code of docker, gcloud and docker-py and found out the following:
1. Docker itself is fully backwards compatible and CAN read from `~/.dockercfg`, but will always write new data to `~/.docker/config.json`
2. Gcloud will write to `~/.dockercfg` using the old file format (plain config object) IF there is no `~/.docker/config.json`. ELSE it will write to `~/.docker/config.json` using the new file format (`auths` object inside config object).
3. docker-py will read from either `~/.dockercfg` or `~/.docker/config.json`, but only uses the new format (see: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/auth.py#L104)

So, if there is no `~/.docker/config.json` and one does `gcloud docker login && gcloud docker -a`, `~/.dockercfg` is created in the old format, which docker-py won't read. Thus, it cannot find the auth credentials and `docker-compose pull` fails.

IMO, docker-py should use the old format for the old file and the new format for the new file, as both docker and Gcloud do.

I worked around this by using `docker login -u _token -p $(gcloud auth print-access-token) eu.gcr.io` for now, but others might run into the same problem.

issue_title: Ability to stop docker-py.events() generator from another thread?
issue_body:
When we want to listen for live docker events with docker-py we have 2 options:

- Fetch events with time limits like `since` and `until`. If `until` is the future date, code is blocking until we meet that date (but not pass over). Minimal time resolution for `since` and `until` is one second.

- Fetch events live without time limits. Generator is blocking until some event pops out.

In projects based on for eg. Python Twisted, we have single event loop thread. We can't run blocking queries, because we don't want to block whole application. That's why we execute blocking code in threads. Each 2 options I mentioned above we have problems.

- Fetching events without time limits can't be stopped. And when whole application is closing thread is still running, so Twisted waits forever for ~thread.join. We have 0 control over generator, because it uses internal waits for some events. We can't use a variable to tell thread that is should be still running. We are totally stuck.

- We are forced to use fetching with time limits. We want to get events live, so we make smallest possible time chunks. Even smallest chunks gives us up to 1 second delay, so it's not live. I don't event mention about logic to manage that chunked fetching

Two scenarios are bad.

Is there something that I'm not aware of, or we really can''t do much more?

issue_title: support for @inlineCallbacks
issue_body:
Would it be possible to support inlineCallbacks?
I would like to do something similar to the code below:

```python
from __future__ import print_function
import txaio
from asyncio.futures import Future
from asyncio.coroutines import coroutine
txaio.use_twisted()
from twisted.internet.defer import Deferred
from twisted.internet.defer import inlineCallbacks
from twisted.internet import reactor


@inlineCallbacks
def test1():
    dfd = Deferred()
    reactor.callLater(1, dfd.callback, 'Test1 OK')

@inlineCallbacks
def test2():
    fut = Future()
    reactor.callLater(1, fut.set_result, 'Test1 OK')

@coroutine
def test3():
    fut = Future()

    print('Test3 started')
    res = yield fut
    print(res)

test1()
test2()
test3()
reactor.run()
```

issue_title: open transformer byte pair indexer should stick its mapping in the vocabulary
issue_body:
our TokenIndexers use the Vocabulary to build up their mappings token <-> index, and so later on if a model needs access to that mapping it can get it through the vocabulary.

the openai transformer byte pair indexer is an oddball in that it uses a precomputed mapping loaded from a file; hence, the corresponding implementation ignores the vocabulary. this means that there's no easy way to access the mapping from e.g. your model.

so probably the byte pair indexer should put its mapping into the vocabulary so that it can be accessed there, same as with every other token indexer

issue_title: Allow for subclassed types
issue_body:
A little background first, in Ansible there is a `json_query` filter plugin which uses `jmespath` to query json. There is an issue open where users of that plugin get the following error:
```
JMESPathTypeError: In function contains(), invalid type for value: irb.1111, expected one of: ['array', 'string'], received: "unknown"
```
The comment that summarizes the issue is here https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/27299#issuecomment-366781707.

The error is caused by the fact that Ansible uses its own types for strings. One of the hacks we've come up with was:
```
jmespath.functions.REVERSE_TYPES_MAP['string'] = jmespath.functions.REVERSE_TYPES_MAP['string'] + ('AnsibleUnicode', 'AnsibleUnsafeText',)
```
which seems weird since I am pretty sure `REVERSE_TYPES_MAP` is not supposed to be public.

is there a proper way to do this? If not, is this something that would be considered to be added?

Thanks!

issue_title: Protect pushes from wrong branch
issue_body:
TLDR; I want to protect certain apps to only be pushable by a certain branch, and preferably not allow forced pushes (similar to gitlabs protected branches - https://about.gitlab.com/2014/11/26/keeping-your-code-protected/).

This to prevent accidental pushes to my production application.

Long story.

Lets say I have an app, and I have different environments (test, preprod, production, various short-lived test branches, ...) for this app. Each of these environments is linked to a branch.
- test -> master
- preprod -> stable
- production -> (also) stable
- feature-x -> feature-x
- you get the idea...

To deploy a new version I just do git push deis-production stable and all is well.
Another developer/ops guy takes an old version of stable, does an emergency commit + deploy and does a git push deis-production stable --force as well.. (In theory he should have checked why he needed to force, but sometines in the heat of time you don't think too well)

Alternatively (and this does not require a --force so is more easy to do accidently) one of the deployment guys does git push deis-production master (either he intended to deploy to test, or intended to deploy another branch).

If there were an option to say deis apps:protect branch-name, to only allow pushes to that application with that branch-name, and enforce non --force pushes, this would prevent these errors

issue_title: Proposal ref(controller): add additional restriction for application name

There is a restriction to use (rfc952)[https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc952] in labels (and the application name is one)

```
Regex: "[a-z]([-a-z0-9]*[a-z0-9])?"
Maximum length: 24
```

issue_title: globus transfer --batch: accept filename
issue_body: Currently globus transfer --batch is defined to take the list of input files on stdin.  Rather than forcing stdin, why not define it as "--batch INPUTFILE", where INPUTFILE can be "-" to mean stdin?  That seems somewhat more flexible and easier, if my file list is in a file.

issue_title: Add `--(no-)managed-endpoint` flag or equivalent to `endpoint update`
issue_body:
Eric Blau raised this as an issue, and we should be sure to track it somewhere.

Currently, XSEDE is relying on the hosted CLI's
```shell
$ endpoint-modify <endpoint name> --managed-endpoint
```

This is the kind of functionality that seems reasonable to add to `endpoint update`, perhaps under a new name, but there is no API support.
If we can get an API (and corresponding SDK) resource to use, we should add this.

I'm partial to `--managed/--no-managed` as the flag, since `-endpoint` seems redundant.

issue_title: Allow setting common arbitrary context properties
process_template_response must return an HttpResponse.
issue_body:
function "process_template_response" must return an HttpResponse. The code below returns None.

Error Point : https://github.com/Microsoft/ApplicationInsights-Python/blob/master/applicationinsights/django/middleware.py#L215

Ref django code : https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/handlers/base.py#L150

Here's what you can change:

```python
def process_template_response(self, request, response):
    if hasattr(request, 'appinsights') and hasattr(response, 'template_name'):
        data = request.appinsights.request
        data.properties['template_name'] = response.template_name

    return response  # FIX POINT
```

Thanks.

issue_title: Implement `State` object
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Currently, the `VM` class is part of the constructor for the `Computation` object.  Here are the places where it is currently used.

```
evm/logic/block.py:7:    block_hash = computation.vm.get_ancestor_hash(block_number)
evm/logic/block.py:13:    computation.stack.push(computation.vm.block.header.coinbase)
evm/logic/block.py:17:    computation.stack.push(computation.vm.block.header.timestamp)
evm/logic/block.py:21:    computation.stack.push(computation.vm.block.header.block_number)
evm/logic/block.py:25:    computation.stack.push(computation.vm.block.header.difficulty)
evm/logic/block.py:29:    computation.stack.push(computation.vm.block.header.gas_limit)
evm/logic/system.py:87:    computation.vm.logger.debug(
evm/logic/system.py:141:            computation.vm.logger.debug(
evm/vm/forks/spurious_dragon/utils.py:22:        yield computation.vm.block.header.coinbase
evm/vm/computation.py:76:        self.vm = vm
evm/vm/computation.py:197:            child_computation = self.vm.apply_create_message(child_msg)
evm/vm/computation.py:199:            child_computation = self.vm.apply_message(child_msg)
evm/vm/computation.py:280:        with self.vm.state_db(read_only, self.msg.access_list) as state_db:
```

Giving computation direct access to the `VM` is problematic for a number of reasons.

- poor isolation for what data and APIs are available during EVM execution.
- makes implementation of stateless clients difficult.
- makes VM execution inherently non-pure.

### How can it be fixed

To fix this, lets create a new `State` object.  Similar to how the `Transaction` class is specified for each `VM`, the `State` object should be similarly overrideable.

- `VM.get_state_class()`
- `VM.get_state()`
- `VM.state` (this could be a convenience `property` that just delegates to `VM.get_state()`.

The `State` object will need the following things.

- Access to the current block header information (`coinbase/timestamp/block_number/difficulty/gas_limit`.
- The ancestry hashes for the current block number.
- Access to the `StateDB`

The `State` object will also need the following APIs available to it.

- `VM.apply_message`
- `VM.apply_create_message`

I think the best way to accomplish this is to relocate the following APIs to live directly on the `State` object itself, removing them from the `VM` object.

- `VM.apply_message`
- `VM.apply_create_message`
- `VM.apply_computation`

Note that `VM.apply_computation` needs access to `VM.get_opcode_fn()` and `VM.precompiles`.  This indicates we'll need to supply the `State` object with a way to get at this data.  The *simplest* solution that comes to mind is to add them as extra arguments:

```python
class State:
    def apply_computation(self, message, opcodes, precompiles):
        ...
```

issue_title: Web3.py excepts ASCII/UTF-8 input instead of HEX for personal.sign.
issue_body: * Version: web3==4.0.0b4 (faced the same on older versions too) * Python: 3.6.3 * OS: osx ### What was wrong? There's a difference in the input format requirements between web3.py and web3.js for the methods `web3.personal.sign` and `web3.personal.ecRecover` (and probably others). I couldn't find a "standard" as such that defined what the correct params should be and am guessing that web3.js is considered as the de-facto standard so the deviation is in web3.py. https://gitter.im/ethereum/web3.py?at=5a3fba255355812e571247d6 I'm running geth `1.7.3-stable` as follows: ``` geth --dev ``` In python: ``` from web3 import Web3, IPCProvider web3 = Web3(IPCProvider('/path/to/geth.ipc')) message1 = '0x68656C6C6F' signature = web3.personal.sign(message1, web3.eth.coinbase, '') verified_address = web3.personal.ecRecover(message1, signature) print('Address matches: ', web3.eth.coinbase.lower() == verified_address.lower(), ' ', verified_address) ``` Output: `Address matches: True 0x08beac2d1f1493f7f477f042f8d2dedcad7ac1f8` However, on running ecRecover in the browser using that signature I get a different address. ``` > web3.personal.ecRecover('0x68656C6C6F', '0x44972d48e51ceeae93a36696295d7074c1ba797548f92fe88489493dc677ef504d12220561b1109b8cb0956ee67621995d2247fa211f9ca47939bd37abf27e6e1b', console.log) null "0x66c37d74d68f707ea6478549bec2198b5fccfb92" ``` In order to get the correct address in the browser on running ecRecover the input message for `personal.sign` in web3.py should be `hello` instead of `0x68656C6C6F`. ``` message2 = 'hello' signature = web3.personal.sign(message2, web3.eth.coinbase, '') verified_address = web3.personal.ecRecover(message2, signature) verified_address, signature ``` Output: `('0x08beac2d1f1493f7f477f042f8d2dedcad7ac1f8', '0x56db7c790d3ded291592c48a90ef785408c8db59ed7805c4de4255a2fe7c4da5099d6f713c23488dd6f3c633406293117c402089e1119429d69dea590987f26b1c')` In the browser: ``` web3.personal.ecRecover('0x68656C6C6F', '0x56db7c790d3ded291592c48a90ef785408c8db59ed7805c4de4255a2fe7c4da5099d6f713c23488dd6f3c633406293117c402089e1119429d69dea590987f26b1c', console.log) null "0x08beac2d1f1493f7f477f042f8d2dedcad7ac1f8" // Correct address ``` The same is applicable for a message signed using web3.js and being verified using web3.py. Note: web3.js throws an error if the message is not encoded as hex. ### How can it be fixed? Throw an error if the "message" passed to these methods is not hex encoded. Also, it is possible that the library itself is encoding the message as hex (I haven't gone through the code yet) and that should be avoided in order to maintain consistency.

issue_title: Add field name of formatter failure to apply_formatters_to_dict
issue_body:
* Version: 3.x & 4.x
* Python: 2.7/3.4/3.5
* OS: osx/linux/win


### What was wrong?

When applying formatters to a dict, if one of the values fails, it's impossible to tell from the trace which one did.

### How can it be fixed?

Catch ValueError exceptions in `apply_formatters_to_dict` and rethrow with the name of the field that failed.

issue_title: Add custom geth shh module
issue_body:
* Version: 4


### What was wrong?

The geth whisper api has drifted from the globally defined json-rpc api.

See #384 

### How can it be fixed?

Write a custom geth whisper module, noting the drift. It should not be added as a default Web3 submodule.

issue_title: Allow call method to query state of past blocks
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Currently the eth-tester library doesn't support using the `eth_call` method to query the state of past blocks in the blockchain. Given that the new version of web3.py will support querying past blocks in the chain it makes sense to have that functionality available in eth-tester as well.

issue_title: [Bug] conda removes folders from working directory when they share name with environment
issue_body:
conda removes folders from working directory when they share their name with the environment that you want to delete. You can find a minimal reproduction at the tagged commit linked here:

https://github.com/Korijn/condabug/tree/conda-env-remove
# Steps to reproduce

Run the following command from the root of the repository:

``` batch
> conda env create
> conda remove -n hw --all -y
```

_Conda has now deleted not only the `hw` environment but also the `hw` source folder!_

Since `conda env remove`'s behavior was redirect to the same code in a fairly recent release, you can also reproduce the bug like this:

``` batch
> conda env create
> conda env remove -n hw -y
```

You can even cause the behavior after the environment has already been deleted.

issue_title: detect "offline", warn, and build package plan from local cache
issue_body:
tl;dr: avoid borked environments when pkg plan calls for pkg download (but offline, so download is impossible) by trying to create package plan just from local package cache.

When creating an environment while "offline" (e.g. under circumstances where some or all pkg repositories are inaccessible) the user should be warned and given the option to proceed just with locally cached packages in the package plan.  Right now under these circumstances environment creation just fails if the package plan calls for some packages to be downloaded, leaving a borked env directory.

Commentary:

I have a stack (hundreds) of conda packages in my local package cache.  I'm offline and not about to be online for some time.  I want to create a new environment.  Somehow conda has enough offline information to identify that for some dependencies there are newer packages available to satisfy my package requests, but they will need to be downloaded.  I can see this probably isn't going to work, but select "Yes" to proceed with the package plan (what other option do I have?)

The appropriate environment directory is created, but then conda immediately chokes because it can't download the newer dependency packages it has identified as being required for the package plan.  I think "maybe --use-index-cache will solve this", but also no luck.

I'm able to dig around my package cache and see which are the latest versions I have locally of these dependency packages, then explicitly specify these (earlier) versions and hope for the best.  This works.  I get exactly the same versions of the packages I'm actually interested in.  This makes me think there could (and perhaps "should") be a way for conda to identify that it is "offline" and then build a package plan only off the locally available packages.

This seems like it would be a common use case in situations where all packages come from a locally approved package source, or in situations where many anaconda users are often in an "isolated" or "offline" mode.

issue_title: miniconda2 installs Python 3.5.2 when creating new env
issue_body:
# How to recreate the issue:

```bash
### Run the cflinuxfs2 Docker image
docker run -it cloudfoundry/cflinuxfs2 bash
### Install miniconda
wget https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda2-4.1.11-Linux-x86_64.sh
chmod +x Miniconda2-4.1.11-Linux-x86_64.sh
./Miniconda2-4.1.11-Linux-x86_64.sh 
### Download environment.yaml file
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cloudfoundry/python-buildpack/develop/cf_spec/fixtures/miniconda_simple_app_python_2/environment.yml
### Create env 
conda env update -n dep_env -f environment.yml
### Activate env
source activate dep_env
### Check Python version
python --version
```

The python version appears as `Python 3.5.2 :: Continuum Analytics, Inc.` when we expect 2.7.11. We expect Python version 2 because we downloaded and installed miniconda 2. 

Note: we are not sure if this is a bug or a new feature. 

-- @RochesterinNYC

issue_title: Consider allowing additional package formats for conda packages
issue_body:
I did some research about using package formats other than `.tar.bz2` for conda packages. The upshot is there seems to be potential to improve the fetch+extract phase of installing conda packages by ~30% when using algorithms that yield a higher compression ratio and/or faster decompression than bzip2. Here is a notebook summarizing these findings:

https://github.com/aburgm/conda-compression-cmp/blob/master/Analysis.ipynb

My personal favorite is `.tar.xz` since the tooling around it as fairly good, it has been adopted in other open source projects such as the Linux kernel or GNOME for their source code tarballs, and is supported by Python since 3.3. Google's brotli algorithm also seems very competitive.

Happy to hear the community's ideas and thoughts about this. If there is consensus that this is a good idea then I think the first step would be to handle additional package formats in case conda encounters them in an index (`repodata.json`) file. I'd be happy to start contributing in that direction.

@kalefranz This is the promised follow-up from the discussion we had at AnacondaCON.

issue_title: Resolve for newest package version when multiple channels are provided
issue_body:
User request:

When specifying more than one repo channel for a package conda does not grab the latest version. Conda does not concatenate the repodata.json between repos before it finds the package and will resolve to the highest version of the package it first encounters.

Example:
repo1 has ipython 3.2.1
repo2 has ipython 5.1.0

```
conda install --override-channels -c http://repo1url -c http://repo2url ipython
```

If there is a need for a user to get packages from 2 repos it will not get the latest as it resolves ipython on repo1. Is it possible to add a feature where conda can combine the repodata.json to resolve which version is highest and grab the package from that repo?

issue_title: conda-env may update conda while it's still in use

Thanks for the report.  As far as I've been able to tell, these errors were always here, but no logger handler was set up, so these messages were not being shown.  Conda-build recently added logger handlers, and sets these to show warnings and above from conda.  Removing the trash is part of conda, though.  I'm moving this issue to the conda issue tracker.

issue_title: Error when creating new conda environment in offline mode
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to create a new conda environment in offline mode using packages that are already present in the root environment:

```
> conda list | grep python
 ipython                   5.3.0                    py35_0  
 ipython_genutils          0.1.0                    py35_0  
 python                    3.5.2                         0  
 python-dateutil           2.6.0                    py35_0
 > conda create --offline -n offline1 python=3.5.2
 Fetching package metadata .........
 Solving package specifications: .
 
 Package plan for installation in environment /conda/envs/offline1:
 
 The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:
 
     openssl:    1.0.2k-1     
     pip:        9.0.1-py35_1 
     python:     3.5.2-0      
     readline:   6.2-2

issue_title: document bash completion for "source activate"
issue_body:
@nevermindewe commented on [Wed Apr 22 2015](https://github.com/conda/conda-docs/issues/37)

TODO: 
 - make a robust version of bash completion for source activate:
```
_complete_source_activate_conda(){
  if [ ${COMP_WORDS[COMP_CWORD-1]} != "activate" ]
  then
    return 0
  fi
  local current_string=${COMP_WORDS[COMP_CWORD]}
  local env_list=$(conda env list | sed '1d;2d;$d' | awk {'print $1'})
  COMPREPLY=($(compgen -W '${env_list[@]}' -- "$current_string"))
  return 0
}

complete -F _complete_source_activate_conda source
```
 - add usage and installation to docs

issue_title: Minconda install error
issue_body:
Obtained and ran installer with these commands on Ubuntu 16.04:

```
wget -nv  https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
chmod a+x Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -b -p /vagrant/miniconda
```

Installer runs and aborts with this error:

```
PREFIX=/vagrant/miniconda
installing: python-3.6.3-h6c0c0dc_5 ...
Python 3.6.3 :: Anaconda, Inc.
installing: ca-certificates-2017.08.26-h1d4fec5_0 ...
installing: conda-env-2.6.0-h36134e3_1 ...
installing: libgcc-ng-7.2.0-h7cc24e2_2 ...
installing: libstdcxx-ng-7.2.0-h7a57d05_2 ...
installing: libffi-3.2.1-hd88cf55_4 ...
installing: ncurses-6.0-h9df7e31_2 ...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/vagrant/miniconda/pkgs/.install.py", line 618, in <module>
    main2()
  File "/vagrant/miniconda/pkgs/.install.py", line 599, in main2
    link_dist(opts.link_dist)
  File "/vagrant/miniconda/pkgs/.install.py", line 454, in link_dist
    link(prefix, dist, linktype)
  File "/vagrant/miniconda/pkgs/.install.py", line 343, in link
    raise Exception("dst exists: %r" % dst)
Exception: dst exists: '/vagrant/miniconda/share/terminfo/e/eterm'
```

I've googled my heart out and can't figure out what to do to resolve this.  I've installed miniconda using this exact method on several machines (virtual or otherwise) and it's installed and run wonderfully.

issue_title: Feature Request: Install `test/requires` dependencies
issue_body:
It would be very useful to have a command line option to allow for the test dependencies to be also installed into an environment - e.g. something like

```
conda create --include-test-deps -n tmp_test_env TEST_PACKAGE
```

```
conda install --include-test-deps TEST_PACKAGE
```

issue_title: Deactivate error in fish shell under conda 4.4
issue_body:
It complains about an array out of bounds.

Seems like line 72 in conda.fish should read:

eval (eval $_CONDA_EXE shell.fish deactivate)

 instead of

eval (eval $_CONDA_EXE shell.fish deactivate $argv[2..-1])

as this command has no parameters.

Changing this seems to make it work.

issue_title: conda env remove fails with AttributeError: module 'conda.plan' has no attribute 'PREFIX'
issue_body: I tried this on three separate machines with conda 4.1.0. Every machine fails with `AttributeError: module 'conda.plan' has no attribute 'PREFIX'` when running conda env remove -n environment_name or conda env remove -p environment_path. This also happens on my Travis environment now causing my builds to fail. Any ideas?

issue_title: Pileup is wrong with spliced reads
issue_body:
If you have  read spliced across a region, pileup will still happily report reference and query positions
even for positions where the read actually has no aligned base.

Toy example: Should not output anything, outputs [(100, 2), (101, 2)...]

import pysam

sam = pysam.AlignmentFile("shu.bam", 'wb', reference_names = ['chr1'], reference_lengths=[100000])
r = pysam.AlignedSegment()
r.query_name = "test1"
r.seq = "ACCT"
r.qual = "xxxx"
r.pos = 100
r.cigartuples = [ (0,2), (3, 500), (0,2)]
sam.write(r)
sam.close()
pysam.index('shu.bam')

sam = pysam.AlignmentFile('shu.bam')
for p in sam.pileup('chr1', 100, 200, truncate=True, stepper='nofilter'):
    for read in p.pileups:
        print 'reference pos', p.reference_pos, 'query pos', read.query_position

issue_title: Error Reading header
issue_body:
Hi,
I Have a problem with pysam 8.1. 
When I try to read the header of a bam file generated by bwa, pysam gives this error:
In [1]: import pysam
In [2]: sam = pysam.Samfile('merged.sample2.bam')
In [3]: sam.header

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-ac1760b5c470> in <module>()
----> 1 sam.header

/home/peio/devel/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysam/calignmentfile.so in pysam.calignmentfile.AlignmentFile.header.__get__ (pysam/calignmentfile.c:13319)()

ValueError: unknown field code 'LB' in record 'PG'

I think the bug is because in the PG line of the header, bwa writes the command  with <tab> in the RG definition option( as it is specified in the command line):

$ samtools view -H /tmp/merged.sample2.bam|grep "^@PG" |cat -A
@PG^IID:bwa^ILB:ECU1032 /home/jope/tomato_6/reads/raw/ECU1032_1.fastq.gz /home/jope/tomato_6/reads/raw/ECU1032_2.fastq.gz^IVN:0.7.10-r789^ICL:bwa mem -t 120 /home/jope/genomes/tomato/S_lycopersicum_chromosomes.2.50.fa -R @RG^ISM:ECU1032^IPL:illumina^IPN:bwa$

To bypass this error I am removing the PG line of my bams, but I think that it should be resolved in othre way? Do you consider this a bug of pysam or a bug of bwa?
Thanks

p.

issue_title: CLI Enhancement Idea: --continue-on-error
issue_body:
When running `--compile`, it might be desirable for VUnit not to exit out when it first encounters an error, but to continue through all steps of the currently executing process.

For example: `python run.py --compile --continue-on-error`

Examples of how this could be helpful:

- Provides a better idea of how many total files in the project compile vs do not compile (due to regressions, incompatibility between different simulators used, etc). This could be used to collect metrics for the code base.
- Allows you to at least partially compile a project, even though some design objects in that library did not compile correctly. This may be perfectly acceptable -- no need for an entire project to compile if you only depend on a few common packages, plus the testbench you are interested in testing.

This option could also apply to normally running VUnit (in simulation mode), but care and thought would need to be given to the case where a newly made change to a design object that your testbench relies on does not get (successfully) recompiled, and you are simulating the old functionality.

issue_title: Cannot change replica count on closed index
issue_body:
Beginning with ES 1.6, this behavior—which was previously allowed—is now prevented.  An error message results:

```
org.elasticsearch.ElasticsearchIllegalArgumentException: Can't update [index.number_of_replicas] on closed indices [[test_index]] - can leave index in an unopenable state
```

As this _can leave index in an inopenable state,_ this behavior should be prevented for all versions of ES.

issue_title: forceMerge params are out of date
issue_body:
The [elasticsearch-py API docs](http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/api.html#elasticsearch.client.IndicesClient.forcemerge) state:

> Parameters:	
> index – A comma-separated list of index names; use _all or empty string to perform the operation on all indices
> allow_no_indices – Whether to ignore if a wildcard indices expression resolves into no concrete indices. (This includes _all string or when no indices have been specified)
> expand_wildcards – Whether to expand wildcard expression to concrete indices that are open, closed or both., default ‘open’, valid choices are: ‘open’, ‘closed’, ‘none’, ‘all’
> flush – Specify whether the index should be flushed after performing the operation (default: true)
ignore_unavailable – Whether specified concrete indices should be ignored when unavailable (missing or closed)
> max_num_segments – The number of segments the index should be merged into (default: dynamic)
> only_expunge_deletes – Specify whether the operation should only expunge deleted documents
operation_threading – TODO: ?
> wait_for_merge – Specify whether the request should block until the merge process is finished (default: true)

But the [Elasticsearch API docs](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-forcemerge.html) state:

> **max_num_segments** The number of segments to merge to. To fully merge the index, set it to 1. Defaults to simply checking if a merge needs to execute, and if so, executes it.
>
> **only_expunge_deletes** Should the merge process only expunge segments with deletes in it. In Lucene, a document is not deleted from a segment, just marked as deleted. During a merge process of segments, a new segment is created that does not have those deletes. This flag allows to only merge segments that have deletes. Defaults to false. Note that this won’t override the index.merge.policy.expunge_deletes_allowed threshold.
>
> **flush** Should a flush be performed after the forced merge. Defaults to true.

A quick look showed that these 3 parameters have been the only ones acceptable since _Elasticsearch 1.5,_ when it was still called Optimize.

issue_title: exists() should not return always True with ignore=404
issue_body:
When querying a missing document,
`get(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id)`
raises an exception, and
`get(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id, ignore=404)`
returns no result, as expected, without the exception.

But if
`exists(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id)`
raises an exception, as expected,
`exists(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id, ignore=404)`
returns True, i.e. the incorrect result.

Shouldn't `exists(ignore=404)` return the correct answer without the exception?

issue_title: Place the short argument in front of long argument in help document.
issue_body:
Here's what our argument help looks like:
```
Arguments
    --destination-blob -b      [Required]: Name of the destination blob. If the exists, it will be
                                           overwritten.
    --destination-container -c [Required]: The container name.
```

Note that the order of the arguments is long first and then short one follows. It is inconsistent with common linux manuals, in which short arguments are in the front and long arguments are aligned.
```
11:44 $ curl --help
Usage: curl [options...] <url>
Options: (H) means HTTP/HTTPS only, (F) means FTP only
     --anyauth       Pick "any" authentication method (H)
 -a, --append        Append to target file when uploading (F/SFTP)
     --basic         Use HTTP Basic Authentication (H)
     --cacert FILE   CA certificate to verify peer against (SSL)
     --capath DIR    CA directory to verify peer against (SSL)
 -E, --cert CERT[:PASSWD]  Client certificate file and password (SSL)
     --cert-status   Verify the status of the server certificate (SSL)
     --cert-type TYPE  Certificate file type (DER/PEM/ENG) (SSL)
     --ciphers LIST  SSL ciphers to use (SSL)
     --compressed    Request compressed response (using deflate or gzip)
 -K, --config FILE   Read config from FILE
```

The latter format is more readable since there short arguments easily stand out.

issue_title: Create a bundled/versioned installer of the CLI
issue_body:
### Description

An install method should exist that is:
- Reproducible - I should be able to specify a version and always get that version of the CLI (and all related dependencies). This is useful for CI/build server scenarios.
- Scriptable/Non-interactive - CI/build machines cannot accept user prompts so it should be scriptable with command line arguments for options.
- Private & Offline - PyPI may not be available behind a proxy so ideally there'd be one archive I can download and distribute to by CI/build machines that is fully self-contained.
- Configurable - Allow me to set the install location (we can't assume root access to the CI/build server)

This installer could behave much like the `https://aka.ms/installazurecli` script but that's not reproducible or non-interactive.

This issue summarizes https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/3058#issuecomment-298100569 and https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/2659#issue-217906670.

I haven't found a workaround yet.

issue_title: [DNS] 'record-set show' and 'record-set list' don't show sufficient data
issue_body:
Consider:

```
C:\Users\jonatul>az network dns record-set show -g rg1 -z jt-test.com -t A -n cli2 -o table
Name    ResourceGroup      Ttl
------  ---------------  -----
cli2    rg1               3600
```

And

```
C:\Users\jonatul>az network dns record-set list -g rg1 -z jt-test.com  -o table
Name                ResourceGroup      Ttl  Type    Metadata
------------------  ---------------  -----  ------  --------------------------------------
@                   rg1               3600  NS
@                   rg1               3600  SOA     s="fdf"
@                   rg1               3600  MX
*                   rg1                100  A
*                   rg1                100  TXT
*                   rg1                100  AAAA
*                   rg1                100  SRV
a                   rg1                300  A       dept="shopping" env="test"
cccname             rg1                100  CNAME
cli2                rg1               3600  A
```

Problems:

1. In both cases, the DNS records themselves are not shown! This is critical data, it must be shown. The record data can be formatted with 1 line per record (see example/details below).
2. For both `show` and `list`, showing 'ResourceGroup' is pointless and wastes space. They're within a zone, which is in the given RG. It will always have the same value, as specified on the command line. Please remove.
3. Metadata is not shown for the `show` command. It should be. Make it the same as for the `list` command
4. For consistency, I suggest also adding the 'Type' column to the output for the `show` command. I.e. the output of `show` and `list` will then be exactly the same. I know 'Type' is redundant for `show` since it's specified on the command line, but it's a narrow column and so I think it's worth it.  (It is standard practice for DNS records to be listed including their type)
5. Please order the columns as follows: Name, Type, TTL, Records, Metadata. That matches the standard sequence used by other DNS tools in the industry (e.g. 'dig')

Example output:

```
Name         Type   Ttl     Records                           Metadata
------------ ------ ------- --------------------------------- ------------------------------
@            SOA    86400   
@            NS     172800  ns1-01.azure-dns.com               dept="shop" env="test"
                            ns2-01.azure-dns.net
                            ns3-01.azure-dns.org
                            ns4-01.azure-dns.info
*            A      3600    1.2.3.4
                            5.6.7.8
foo          AAAA   60      2001:db8::4
bar          CNAME  0       www.microsoft.com
fish         MX     3600    10 mail1.microsoft.com
                            20 mail2.microsoft.com
worm         PTR    3600    reverse.contoso.com
dog          SRV    1800    10 100 80 foo.bar.com
                            20 200 80 bar.com
cat          TXT    360     "This string is truncated to fit."
                            "This is a second TXT record"
```

Notes:

1. Standard order of fields in SOA record is: email, host, serial, refresh, retry, expire, minTTL. I suggest simply laying them out in this sequence (space-separated) and truncating to fit.
2. In the above example, there are 4 NS records in the record set. This is normal. If they can't be laid out on separate lines, then lay them out on one line (semicolon separated) and truncate to fit.
3. Standard order of fields in MX record is: preference, exchange. Lay them out in this sequence (space separated). Again, I'd prefer one line per record, but if that isn't possible then lay out the records with semi-colon separators and truncated to fit.
4. Standard order of fields in SRV record is: priority, weight, port, target. As above, use spaces between fields, and if multiple records need to be on the same line use semicolons to separate them
5. Each TXT record set can contain multiple TXT records. And each TXT record can contain multiple strings.  **Within** a TXT record, those strings should be concatenated for display purposes. Please also put " " marks around the resulting string.  Separate TXT records should be displayed separately as above.
6. If you can right-align the Ttl, that would be awesome. Be sure to allow enough space for 6-digit Ttls.

issue_title: [Test] Need mechanism to thoroughly test module imports
issue_body:
Recently we released a module whose `setup.py` file was missing some necessary entries in it's packages list, resulting in a non-functioning build. The CI did not catch this because it only ensures that the wheel file builds and that `az` runs. This approach is insufficient because it is not guaranteed to trigger the relevant imports.

When we run a module's tests, we run the `dev_setup.py` script which doesn't rely on the packages list. 

We need to ensure that our CI system will, as best as it can, detect packaging errors like this. Possible solutions:

- include additional metadata in `setup.py` that runs certain commands after wheel installation to ensure that no import errors are thrown. This should be fairly easy to implement but the downside is that not every command which could be chosen is guaranteed to trigger the imports.
- Run the tests using a wheel installation instead of a dev setup.  Since the tests aren't included in the packages, this could be tricky, but would be the most thorough option.

cc/ @mayurid

issue_title: KNACK Storage module conversion
x-ms-client-request-id causes Interactive Mode / ScenarioTest scenarios to fail
issue_body:
Consider the following commands:
```
az network lb create -g myrg -n lb
az network lb inbound-nat-rule create -g myrg --lb-name lb -n rule1 --frontend-port 100 --backend-port 100 --protocol tcp
az network lb inbound-nat-rule create -g myrg --lb-name lb -n rule2 --frontend-port 200 --backend-port 200 --protocol tcp
```

This creates a load balancer with two inbound NAT rules. This scenario runs just fine if you use the CLI normally, and it runs fine in the current scenario test (which uses the `ResourceGroupVCRTestBase` class).

However, if you run these commands in interactive mode, or you run it as part of a `ScenarioTest` based test, the call to create the second rule will fail, stating that the second rule does not exist. Examination via the portal confirms this as well.

issue_title: Block Blobs Limitation in Documentation
issue_body:
According to the block blob [documentation](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/azure-storage-blob/azure/storage/blob/blockblobservice.py) it says:
```
Each block in an append blob can be a different size, up to a maximum of 4 MB,
    and an append blob can include up to 50,000 blocks. The maximum size of an
    append blob is therefore slightly more than 195 GB (4 MB X 50,000 blocks).
```

However, the latest Microsoft [documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/understanding-block-blobs--append-blobs--and-page-blobs) says:
```
The maximum size of a block blob is therefore slightly more than 4.75 TB (100 MB X 50,000 blocks).
For REST versions before 2016-05-31, the maximum size of a block blob is a little more than 195 GB (4 MB X 50,000 blocks).
```

Which REST version is the Python Storage SDK using and can we update the documentation if applicable?

issue_title: Feature request: pre-commit run --sha
issue_body:
I'd like to be able to run pre-commit on a past commit. My use case is two stage CI which I think is fairly common (CircleCI, Gerrit, Travis etc):

1) user submits commit to code host
2) CI system is monitoring code host, sees new commit, checks out commit, runs tests, leaves comment on code host
3) User can merge commit to master once CI has left a message that tests pass
4) After user merges, full integration build starts

In step (2), all that I want to run is```pre-commit run --sha $SHA```, which is substantially faster than ```pre-commit run --all-files```. ```pre-commit run --all-files``` is still run in step (4).

issue_title: check-docstring-first fails positive on attribute docstrings
issue_body:
Attribute docstrings, as [defined in PEP-257](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/#id15) are strings immediately following top level module names.  For example:

```
name = "my variable"
"""the name used by the module for x, y, and z"""
```

The check-docstring-first incorrectly identifies this attribute docstring as a module docstring, indicating either `Multiple module docstrings (first docstring on line N).` or `Module docstring appears after code (code seen on line N).`.

In light of this issue, I'm not sure the `check-docstring-first` check has much value at all. At the very least, the check should not be failing when a docstring follows an assignment.

issue_title: Hook to delete branches that have already been merged into master
issue_body: Not sure if this sort of functionality makes sense to have in a hook, but I figured I'd suggest it and see what people think. issue_title: Assignment List extension is slow
issue_body: The reason is because it actually has to shell out to the command line apps rather than running them in the same python session, and the reason it can't run them in the same python session is because the `NotebookApp` and `NbGrader` both inherit from `JupyterApp` which is a singleton, so it raises an error if you try to instantiate `NbGrader` within the `NotebookApp`. Fixing this would require a fair bit of refactoring: moving the bulk of the transfer apps into separate classes that are not subclasses of `JupyterApp`, and then having the apps themselves create those classes and call them (similarly to how the preprocessors work in nbconvert).

issue_title: Temporary disabling autosave / in-memory copy of a notebook
issue_body:
Hi,

Very often, I open a notebook which is in a stable state, and want to rerun some cells with some tweaks with some variables or something like this, but mostly in a prototype way, and I want to discard the result afterwards.

If I open my notebook and directly edit it, the autosaving feature stores the changes definitely, and I lose my previous values and parameters.

If I clone the notebook, I need to have a copy in the same folder, which is not practical either for a temporary test.


So I propose to either offer a menu entry to disable autosave feature temporarily. As an (even better?) alternative, it would be awesome to be able to have in-memory copy of a notebook. Then, "copy" notebook menu entry would make an in-memory copy, and the user would be free to save it whenever he wants.

Thanks!

I noticed some performance issues in Firefox when I recently upgraded to the Jupyter notebook (4.0.5) from IPython Notebook (2.4.1). import logging
import sys

```

I noticed this sluggish behavior because the logging and clearing function is used in a third party module I use for analysis however I created a notebook that gives me a similar result (attached same as above). I have tested this in Firefox and Firefox in safe mode. Google Chrome works fine.

issue_title: iopub rate limits are too low by default, for visualization-heavy projects
issue_body:
After upgrading to Jupyter version 5.0.0b1 on Mac OS X, I'm getting errors like:

```
IOPub data rate exceeded.
The notebook server will temporarily stop sending output
to the client in order to avoid crashing it.
To change this limit, set the config variable
`--NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit`.
```

for just about every notebook I run, all of which have various visualizations.  From that message I had no idea what units the rate limits might be expressed in, but by adding enough zeros I eventually figured out that the problems go away if I launch jupyter with:

```
jupyter notebook --NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit=10000000000
```

As mentioned in #1821, it is not obvious what limits are reasonable to set for this value by default, but I would like to put in my vote that the current value is at least an order of magnitude too low.  A sample matplotlib script that usually causes the problem is below, but I mostly use HoloViews, where I see the problem for just about every visualization when using the Bokeh backend, which requires a lot of JavaScript to be sent to the browser.  

As someone who in part maintains HoloViews, I now have to recommend that all of our users start Jupyter in this way, which makes it very awkward for them, for what I think is a very reasonable way to be using a Jupyter notebook for visualizations.  So I would strongly plead with you to increase this value by default so that it is only reached for actual error cases, not for ordinary visualization workflows.


```
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

freq = 100 
def sine(x, phase=0, freq=freq):
    return np.sin((freq * x + phase))

dist = np.linspace(-0.5,0.5,256)
x,y = np.meshgrid(dist, dist)
grid = (x**2+y**2)

testpattern = sine(grid, freq=freq)
methods = [None, 'none', 'nearest', 'bilinear', 'bicubic', 'spline16',
           'spline36', 'hanning', 'hamming', 'hermite', 'kaiser', 'quadric',
           'catrom', 'gaussian', 'bessel', 'mitchell', 'sinc', 'lanczos']

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 6, figsize=(96, 48))
fig.subplots_adjust(hspace=0.3, wspace=0.05)

for ax, interp_method in zip(axes.flat, methods):
    ax.imshow(testpattern , interpolation=interp_method, cmap='gray')
    ax.set_title(interp_method)

plt.show()
```

issue_body:
Since the recent revisions to the core container types don't seem to be compatible with taking a slice (as opposed to just a single element). As a minimal example:
```python
>>> from autograd import grad
>>> def f(x):
...     s = slice(None, -1, None)
...     y = x[s]
...     return y[0]
... 
>>> grad(f)([1., 2., 3.])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py", line 26, in gradfun
    return vjp(1.0)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 18, in <lambda>
    return lambda g : backward_pass(g, end_node, start_node), end_node
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 37, in backward_pass
    cur_outgrad = vsum(node.vspace, *outgrads[node])
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 131, in vsum
    return primitive_vsum(vspace, *args)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 70, in __call__
    result_value = self.fun(*argvals, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 138, in primitive_vsum
    ans = arg.mut_add(ans)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/container_types.py", line 30, in mut_add
    result[idx] = vs.shape[idx].mut_add(result[idx], x)
AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'mut_add'
```
but anything where you take more than one list element in one go will break, e.g.

```
This breaks the varational_autoencoder example (which is how I found the bug). In particular line 48:
```python
    for W, b in params[:-1]:
```
I can try to work out a fix for this but thought I would raise it in case one of you guys knows how to fix it real quick 😉.

issue_body:
Hi Peter,

In pie client,  KmipOperationFailure will be raised if the operation failed. 
I have seen in exceptions.py, you combined status, reason and message into one message string for KmipOperationFailure. Then how the caller is supposed to handle the exception based on different reasons? By parsing the message string?
Should it be more reasonable to define it like this:
```
  class KmipOperationFailure(Exception):
    def __init__(self, status, reason, message):
        super(KmipOperationFailure, self).__init__(message)
        self.status = status
        self.reason = reason

issue_title: toggle_state default for active is error-prone
issue_body:
`toggle_state` takes an optional `active` param that lets you specify whether the state should be on or off, instead of it being switched from its previous value.  Having a default value of `None` is problematic because `None` can easily be mistaken for a "truthy" `False` when dealing with relation data, which leads to the state being unexpectedly set.  For example, the following breaks:

```python
conv.toggle_state('{relation_name}.feature_x',
                  active=conv.get_remote('feature_x_available'))
```

This can also be more subtle because `None and <anything>` returns `None` instead of `False` as one might expect.  For example, this also breaks:

```python
name = conv.get_remote('name')
version = conv.get_remote('version')
conv.toggle_state('{relation_name}.ready', name and version)
```

issue_title: Framework tries to run all files marked as executable and crashes on .md and .yaml files
issue_body:
Hi


If an interface layer contains files such as README.md that are marked as executable, then you get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "hooks/install", line 19, in <module>
    main()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/charms/reactive/__init__.py", line 67, in main
    bus.dispatch()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/charms/reactive/bus.py", line 397, in dispatch
    hook_handlers = _test(Handler.get_handlers())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/charms/reactive/bus.py", line 381, in _test
    return list(filter(lambda h: h.test(), to_test))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/charms/reactive/bus.py", line 381, in <lambda>
    return list(filter(lambda h: h.test(), to_test))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/charms/reactive/bus.py", line 332, in test
    proc = subprocess.Popen([self._filepath, '--test'], stdout=subprocess.PIPE, env=os.environ)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/subprocess.py", line 859, in __init__
    restore_signals, start_new_session)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/subprocess.py", line 1457, in _execute_child
    raise child_exception_type(errno_num, err_msg)
```

I think it's logical that the framework tries to execute all files. However, I think it should show an error message that is a lot clearer. I think this is a problem that a lot of new people will run into. Marking all files as executable is something that new people often do. I don't think we should guard them from mistakes like this but the least we can do is better explain to them what is wrong. If you agree, I can create a patch that returns a clearer error message.


Kind regards
Merlijn

Research Subnet.tftp
issue_body:
The semantics of the `Subnet.dhcp` field are not well understood. As a result, the field is omitted. The API documentation mentions it, though. From the API documentation:

> POST /api/subnets
> subnet[tftp_id]
> Proxy TFTP para usar nesta subrede
>  Value: number.

Research the semantics of this field and add it to `nailgun.entities` if appropriate.

issue_title: failure when using Id token
issue_body:
While looking at SatelliteQE/betelgeuse#65 I looked at the betelgeuse code and I noticed in add_test_case:

'''
371         # Fetch the test case id if the @Id tag is present otherwise generate a
372         # test_case_id based on the test Python import path
'''

So, I thought I'd try to use that to set the test case "title" in polarion:

'''
# cat test_spoore_betelgeuse_01.py 
class IpaBetelgeuseExampleClass(object):
    def test_betelgeuse_example_0001(self):
        """ @Id: IDM-IPA-TC: IPA Betelgeuse Example 0001
            @Description: New test 0001
        """
        pass
'''
But, I see this error:
'''
# betelgeuse test-case --path test_spoore_betelgeuse_01.py RedHatEnterpriseLinux7
Updating test case test_betelgeuse_example_0001 for requirement Spoore Betelgeuse 01.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/betelgeuse", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('Betelgeuse==0.4.0', 'console_scripts', 'betelgeuse')()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/core.py", line 716, in __call__
    return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/core.py", line 696, in main
    rv = self.invoke(ctx)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/core.py", line 1060, in invoke
    return _process_result(sub_ctx.command.invoke(sub_ctx))
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/core.py", line 889, in invoke
    return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/core.py", line 534, in invoke
    return callback(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click-6.6-py2.7.egg/click/decorators.py", line 17, in new_func
    return f(get_current_context(), *args, **kwargs)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/betelgeuse.py", line 625, in test_case
    pool.join()
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 250, in map
    return self.map_async(func, iterable, chunksize).get()
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 554, in get
    raise self._value
AssertionError
'''

That error, I think is related to the test_case_id including spaces.  I added a simple patch to my local copy to add quotes:
'''
diff --git a/betelgeuse.py b/betelgeuse.py
index 3c2a035..c0f4d86 100644
--- a/betelgeuse.py
+++ b/betelgeuse.py
@@ -414,6 +414,7 @@ def add_test_case(args):
 
         results = []
         if not collect_only:
+            test_case_id = '"{}"'.format(test_case_id)
             results = TestCase.query(
                 test_case_id,
                 fields=[
'''
That seemed to resolve the issue I was seeing.

But, it appears as if the test case was still created with the method name instead of the 'Id' token value.

issue_title: DataCollector carrying over information
issue_body:
We recently did an adaptation of the Wolf Sheep example to try and build a disease model (susceptible, infected, recovered) in mesa. When we plotted the population sizes, as is done in the wolf sheep model, the chart plots correctly the first time. However, when we reset the parameters and try and run a new model, the chart that shows up in the server **first** displays the data from the previous model's execution, and **then** it shows the data from the next iteration. 

An example is shown below:

![screen_shot 2018-04-17 at 4 27 27 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25071352/38894956-b6718970-425c-11e8-8ce5-052b22182265.jpg)

The sharp discontinuity in the graph is where the new data for the model starts being displayed. We then thought that it may have been our model (a bug), but revisiting the wold sheep model we saw the same thing

![screen_shot 2018-04-17 at 4 27 34 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25071352/38895039-f41f8efc-425c-11e8-894d-18b155dd6c88.jpg)

Is there a known issue where the DataCollector does not reset between runs? Closing the kernel and restarting it fixes the issue.

Our code can be found [here](https://github.com/nathawkins/CMSE202--Semester-Project/tree/master/agent_based_virus).

issue_title: LoadScopeScheduling._pending_of slow for many tests
issue_body:
As each test is marked complete _pending_of is called at least once.  _pending_of then iterates over a dictionary that contains a True/False for every assigned test.  This has n^2 performance in the number of tests, which can really have an effect when you have a few thousand tests.  

2 simple fixes come to mind:
1) Keep a dictionary of {node : pending_count} and increment/decrement as necessary.  Then _pending_of is just a lookup instead of an iteration.
2) Delete finished nodeids and scopes from self.assigned_work to make the iteration faster.

Thoughts?  Option 1 seems preferable to me, as then you're still keeping track of finished tests.

issue_title: Proposal: control test distribution in pytest-xdist
event loop is closed before my fixture's finalizer runs
issue_body:
Consider this:

``` python
import pytest


async def foo():
    print("foo")


@pytest.yield_fixture
def my_fixture(event_loop):
    print(event_loop.is_closed()) # "False"
    event_loop.run_until_complete(foo()) # "foo"

    yield None

    print(event_loop.is_closed()) # "True"... noooo!
    event_loop.run_until_complete(foo()) # goes boom


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_foo(my_fixture):
    await foo()
```

Run this code with `py.test -s <file>` and you'll see the output as indicated by the comments in the code.

The event loop is closed before my finalizer code can run. This is also the case when I write my fixture without a yield, instead adding a finalizer function.

This is weird. I'm pretty sure that this worked just fine a couple of months ago.

issue_title: Webroot test does not remove .well-known
issue_body:
I created certificates for several of my domains and noticed that letsencrypt left behind an empty `.well-known` directory in my webroot. This happens because it uses `os.makedirs` to create `{root_path}/URI_ROOT_PATH`, with `URI_ROOT_PATH` being `.well-known/acme-challenges`. When it cleans up, it removes the single directory `root_path/URI_ROOT_PATH`, but it doesn't remove *each level* of the URI_ROOT_PATH it created.

I attempted a patch here which "steps up" each level of URI_ROOT_PATH and attempts to delete them: https://github.com/othatbrian/letsencrypt/commit/74dd1fc7d502d1a1de3881e7be0bd13df9b3c86f
but I haven't set up a test environment to attempt to test that particular fix.

issue_title: Better handling of ack requests
issue_body:
Sometimes at any random point in time, an `ack` request may be received, thus considering the invoked method to be confirmed. This will end the loop that checks whether the result was received yet or not, thus having a `None` result and failing at random points on the code.

The problem however is that there are some requests which do depend on the `ack` request to confirm the invoked method, such as the `LogoutRequest`. How to tell which should be confirmed and which shouldn't doesn't seem trivial, furthermore, this happens randomly (although it is more likely to happen if sending several `GetFileRequest`'s, personal experience).

Maybe one should look into the `tdesktop` source to check how they handle this kind of stuff.

issue_title: Hide importlib stack in visualization?
issue_body:
Since the switch to pure-python importlib, importing a module brings in a bunch of functions to the call stack (`_find_and_load`, _find_and_load_unlocked`, `_load_unlocked`, `exec_module`, `_call_with_frames_removed`, etc.)  Typically this shouldn't be an issue, except when profiling a startup where import time is significant.
Perhaps these should be special-cased and hidden away (like for traceback display)?

issue_title: Feature Request: Ability sync data between 2 b2 buckets
issue_body:
It may be desirable at some point to make a new b2 bucket as a b2 'backup' before some destructive change to a local source replicating to a destination b2 bucket

To make this 'backup' on the b2 server side, the sync command could be used to replicate the data before making any local changes which may get replicated in an undesirable fashion.

The issue currently however, is that the b2 sync command expects 1 of the 'source' or 'destination' folders to be a local folder:

```
-bash-4.1# b2 sync b2://564dcdc2-4a25-fc44-af2a-b369d22dfa7d/* b2://564df3d9-26c9-bc34-6e42-a042e0948d28/

Both s3cmd and gsutil are capable of syncing between buckets server side.

issue_title: Support custom Marshmallow fields
issue_body: Currently, all custom fields automatically become `type="string", format=None` due to the [hard-coded `FIELD_MAPPING`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/bc6e0e5df8d23fd7dadedd09339f1e76910c2498/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L24-L42). Thus, I cannot extend any marshmallow type, e.g. `Dict` or `Number` with apispec support. Currently, the only way to extend apispec support is to patch the `FIELD_MAPPING` dict.

issue_title: Marshmallow schema parameters are resolved using schema class, not schema instance
issue_body:
When apispec resolves parameters using marshmallow schemas, it appears to use the Schema *class*, not the Schema *instance*:

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/__init__.py#L146

I believe this is problematic because Marshmallow allows for schema modifiers on instantiation, but these modifiers are therefore ignored by apispec.  This is important because different uses of a schema may require different instantiations.

For example, say you define a schema like so:

```python
class SomeEntitySchema(Schema):
    id = fields.Integer(required=True)
    name = fields.String(required=True)
```

On a get-item, either the class or the generic instantiation `SomeEntitySchema()` works.  

However, on a post, you would use it as a body param like `SomeEntitySchema(exclude=(id,)` -- ie when you're creating a new entity, you don't pass in the ID (this would be assigned by the server).

So the bug is since apispec uses the *class* of the schema not the *instance* in the parameter case, instance-specific modifiers are ignored.  The apispec tells you that `id` is required on a post, which you specifically said it's not.

Any insight into why https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/__init__.py#L146 looks up the class instead of using the instance?

issue_title: Dynamically specify 'only' parameter for nested fields
issue_body:
I'm able to specify the `only` parameter when instantiating a schema (`MySchema(only=('field1', 'field2')`), however, this does not work for nested fields. I'd like to be able to have something like `MySchema(only=('field1', ('field2', ('subfield1', 'subfield3'))))` where *field2* is a nested field of which only *subfield1* and *subfield2* should be serialized.

I'd be willing to implement it, however, first I wanna know if it has a chance to get into marshmallow. Secondly, it seems to not be a straight forward addition; instead of (or additionally to) during instantiation of the nested schema, the *only* parameter needs to be passed when serializing. So I'd appreciate any hints of how to implement this properly.

The motivation behind this are APIs like [JSONAPI](http://jsonapi.org/) which allow to dynamically specify fields like so: `GET /articles?include=author&fields[articles]=title,body&fields[people]=name`. One could argue that resources are not really rendered in a nested fashion in JSONAPI responses. So lets assume, we use a (not yet implemented) `IncludeSchema` instead. That will, however, leave us with the same challenges of specifying the *only* parameter dynamically.

issue_title: Include Ordered Set as a package instead of writing it yourself?
issue_body:
I studied marshmellows code, since I was looking for a project, that I understand well enough, in order to start contributing to it. I really like marshmellow and I feel that its code and documentation are very well written. One thing, that I noticed though are the custom written collectibles. I understand that marshmellow takes its custom OrderedDict only for Python 2.6, where there is no other choice, but for the custom OrderedSet there would be other choices: I found two packages: 
1. the first is very similar to the function used now: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ordered-set. It is written in pure Python, but has some improvements that are all stated on the projects github page. It seems more clean and performs better.
2. the second is written in Cython: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/orderedset. It comes with better documentation then the first package and differs more form the current solution.
Both are based on Raymond Hettinger's OrderedSet recipe, just as the one used now is. I would suggest to use one of these two options instead of the custom OrderedSet. 
If the community agrees I would be happy to work on this. What do you think about this suggestion?

Not sure if this is a different issue to https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/issues/207 as the modified version of miniterm indicated in that thread doesn't seem to resolve the issue.

On Mac and Linux, miniterm seems to be fine, launched as documented here...
https://vgkits.org/blog/connecting-with-miniterm/
...and the arrow keys work fine on Mac and Linux too.

However, the Windows invocation documented there creates a console session in which arrow keys and backspace keys are junked.

The invocation I tried most recently on Windows (note this was an attempt to use the 'patched miniterm-MPY') was...

```bash
python miniterm_mpy.py --raw --eol CRLF --encoding ascii COM3 115200
```

This leads to a terminal in which pressing the up key produces first...

```
?H?
```

...and then if you press again it seems to send an up arrow key and shows...

```
 clearPixel()
```

...then if you press again it shows 

```
 clearPixel()[12DsetPixel(0, red)
```
...and finally...
```
 clearPixel()[12DsetPixel(0, red)[16D[K4+4
```

What it should show is single, clear lines drawn from the history one by one as you press up and down like...

```
clearPixel()
setPixel(0, red)
4+4
```

I'd really like to know that I don't have to guide users to run everything inside the Ubuntu subsystem on Windows 10 just to get a terminal to work, but it sounds like that might be true.

state.stack.append(BitVec("calldata_" + str(environment.active_account.contract_name) + "_" + str(op0), 256)) -> calldata_4 + 32 **better** calldata[4+32] **or** calldata_at[4+32] **with simplify** calldata[36] and calldata_at[36] respectively

issue_title: Remove references to deprecated patterns() function
Unset value on ToOneField makes it None
issue_body:
If there's a foreign key on a resource, and the bundle data doesn't include that field, the foreign key field gets set to None during the hydration process, even if it already has a value.

The following test case illustrates this (and also how it behaves differently from a non-relational field)

```python
def test_full_hydrate__does_not_overwrite_related_value_if_not_put(self):
        class RelatedBasicResource(BasicResource):
            parent = fields.ToOneField(BasicResource, 'parent', null=True, blank=True)
        basic = RelatedBasicResource()
        basic_bundle_1 = Bundle(data={
            'name': 'Daniel',
            'date_joined': None,
        })
        basic_bundle_1.obj = Mock()
        basic_bundle_1.obj.date_joined = aware_datetime(2010, 2, 15, 12, 0, 0)
        basic_bundle_1.obj.view_count = 3
        basic_bundle_1.obj.parent = Mock()

        self.assertNotEqual(basic_bundle_1.obj.view_count, None)
        self.assertNotEqual(basic_bundle_1.obj.parent, None)

        # Now load up the data.
        hydrated = basic.full_hydrate(basic_bundle_1)

        self.assertNotEqual(hydrated.obj.view_count, None)
        self.assertNotEqual(hydrated.obj.parent, None)
```

I believe https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/commit/73425c31ce732646c6b2299feba4b913d9e93387 is the cause of this. I see the problem in 0.13.3 and the latest repo version but not in 0.13.2.

The field is both blank and null but it now hits the null case before the blank case and therefore gets set to None rather than falling through. I don't know tastypie well enough to have the right solution but hopefully this information can shed some light for somebody :)

issue_title: release version 0.3.0
issue_body:
I'd like to release version 0.3.0 in the next week or two. The idea is to get the new API and new documentation out in the wild sooner than later. New features can come later, and I changed some milestones to reflect this. issue_title: reorder function arguments to put variables first and parameters second
issue_body:
I think we should reorder a handful of function arguments to put the most variable-like arguments first and most parameter-like arguments last. I'm guessing that a lot people will think that I'm just screwing with the API for no reason, but I do have a few reasons!

1. consistency is important (and underrated)
2. we can make better use of default arguments. 

Here are the functions that I know about so far:

```python
ashraeiam(b, aoi) --> ashraeiam(aoi, b=0.05)

physicaliam(K, L, n, aoi) --> physicaliam(aoi, K=4., L=0.002, n=1.526)

singlediode(module, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth) -->
singlediode(photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth, module)

# I just noticed that the matlab sapm api is very different, so that's another issue
sapm(module, poa_direct, poa_diffuse, temp_cell, airmass_absolute, aoi) --> 
sapm(poa_direct, poa_diffuse, temp_cell, airmass_absolute, aoi, module)

snlinverter(inverter, v_dc, p_dc) --> snlinverter(v_dc, p_dc, inverter)
```

issue_title: Create FactoryAggregate provider
issue_body:
Idea is to create provider that would aggregate factories and provide dynamic interface for creating different types of objects:

```python
"""`FactoryAggregate` providers example."""

import sys

import dependency_injector.providers as providers

from games import Chess, Checkers, Ludo


game_factory = providers.FactoryAggregate(chess=providers.Factory(Chess),
                                          checkers=providers.Factory(Checkers),
                                          ludo=providers.Factory(Ludo))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    game_type = sys.argv[1].lower()

    selected_game = game_factory.create(game_type)
    selected_game.play()

    # $ python example.py chess
    # Playing chess

    # $ python example.py checkers
    # Playing checkers

    # $ python example.py ludo
    # Playing ludo
```

Also such aggregates could be used for representing families of related object factories.

issue_title: __module__ of classes imported from __init__.py is confusing for sphinx
issue_body:
Sphinx (using [intersphinx](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/ext/intersphinx.html)) can't find some classes from your objects.inv, resulting in 

```text
WARNING: py:class reference target not found: dependency_injector.containers.declarative.DeclarativeContainer

because it is referenced as `dependency_injector.containers.DeclarativeContainer` and not `dependency_injector.containers.declarative.DeclarativeContainer` in the [objects.inv](http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/objects.inv)
