issue_title: a special max-sat problem: maximizing the number of predefined assignments
issue_body:
I have some questions concerning the capabilities of Z3's tactics and tacticals.

The problems for which we are using SMT technology have the following characteristic:

(1) There is a base SMT-problem (CSP) that contains all the obligatory constraints that must be obeyed. The base CSP is guaranteed to be sat (unless there is an error somewhere). Thus when solving such a CSP, we are not interested in answering the question whether it is sat or unsat or unknown, but we want to obtain a solution (model) for the CSP.

(2) In addtion to generating a base solution we are generating several extra solutions. For each variable (Boolean, Int, Real, ...) occuring in the CSP we have a set of special values that we would like to squeeze in into one of these extra solutions. Of course, not every special value is compatible with the CSP's constraints. Some special values are impossible. Some special values are incompatible with other special values, and therefore cannot occur in the same extra solution.

(3) We want to minimize the number of extra solutions. Thus we have to solve an optimization problem. The minimum number of solutions that we are looking for does not have to be a mathematically strict absolute minimum. An approximate minimum is sufficient for our purposes.

Using a standard SMT-solver we create the desired CSP-solutions in the following way:

The algorithm works with several runs, one extra solution per run. A run is started when there is at least one variable (left) that has an unused special value.

(a) Via check-sat and get-model  a base solution is computed. The solution is read out and saved in a cache. The solver context is pushed.

(b) The next variable with at least one unused special value is selected. If there is no such variable left, the cached solution is written to disk, and the run terminates.

(c) One of the variable's special values is selected. The value is assigned to the variable using a normal assert. This is a voluntary constraint that is present in addition to the obligatory constraints.

(d) A check-sat is perfomed. 
- In case of sat, the current solution is read out via get-model and is saved in the cache. The special value is marked as used and is removed from the variable's set of special values. The solver context is pushed. 
- In case of unsat the context is popped. (We have no experience with the result unknown yet.) When the variable is the first variable in the current run, the special value is removed, because it is impossible. Otherwise it is put back into the variable's set of special values. Then we continue with step (b) .

The algorithm is deterministic and is guaranteed to terminate.

As an optimization, for each variable we also mark a special value as used if it accidentally occurs in a solution.

Obviously, our problem is similar to a max-sat problem, except that we have two sets of constraints, namely one set with obligatory constraints, and one set with voluntary constraints. The former are static and stay, whereas the latter are dynamic, and may be present and fulfilled or not.

Here are my questions:

(1) is it possible to internalize the deterministic algorithm above using Z3's current set of tactics and tacticals? 

(2) If not, what would be missing? Would it be possible to conceive one or more tactics or tacticals that, in cooperation with tactics and tacticals already existing in Z3, would solve the above optimization problem? In my view, it would be sufficient to internalize a single run into Z3.

(3) Could a stochastic local search (SLS) algorithm be used for the optimization? I am thinking of equating an extra Boolean variable with each of the extra equations. The optimization would mean to maximize in each run the number of these extra Boolean variables that are true. Over time the number of Boolean variables that need to be considered would diminish from run to run, until none is left over.

from z3 import *

def equation(expr=None):

issue_title: Insufficiently enthusiastic about emitting header table size shrinkages.
issue_body:
Whenever we shrink the table size in the encoder, we *must* emit a header table size update field. This is true even if we change the header table size multiple times: e.g., we shrink and then raise the header table size. Currently we don't do this, which means we're technically violating the RFC.

This came from the following comment on the ML:

> - If between headers decoder reduces the limit below size signaled by
>  encoder, the encoder must first reduce the table size to the minimum
>  it was between the frames or less (it can then increase it up to
>  current limit).
> 
> As example of the last point:
> ```
> [4k dynamic table size in use]
> --> HEADERS
> <-- SETTINGS(SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=4k)
> <-- SETTINGS(SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=2k)
> <-- SETTINGS(SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=4k)
> <-- SETTINGS(SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=8k)
> <-- SETTINGS(SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=6k)
> --> HEADERS
> ```
>  
> The second HEADERS must first reduce the dynamic table to at most
> 2k. It can then increase dynamic table size to up to 6k.

issue_title: HDFSFile
issue_body:
Dear developers,

As requested on the mailing list I will try to file the feature request to the best of my knowledge.
Some of the functionality requested is already present with `MassStorageFile`.

The requirement for the new file type, the HDFSFile: needs to understand the different between the POSIX mount and the distributed storage.

For convenience the distributed storage is available as a POSIX mount on `/hdfs` (or `/mnt/hadoop` on some sites) which works well for **read** operations.
However, **write** operations can be a bit tricky (e.g. no random writes in HDFS) and "taxing" on the POSIX mount. Therefore it is better to use the built-in commands `hadoop fs -mkdir` and `hadoop fs -copyFromLocal` instead (`MassStorageFile` can do this). These commands take the DFS path (i.e. `/hdfs/myfolder` becomes `/myfolder`).

So an HDFSFile should
 - have a configurable POSIX mount path (e.g.  `/hdfs` or `/mnt/hadoop`)
 - write to HDFS with the appropriate commands
 - return POSIX mount paths for read
 - if an output file is an HDFSFile it should be written locally first, then copied to HDFS and finally the local copy has to be deleted.

All in all, this is barely a convenience feature as most cases are covered by `MassStorageFile`.

Cheers,
Luke

issue_title: Consistency on PUT with mandatory schema fields
issue_body:
While working on #790 I realize that there is something not clear in our specifications.

Currently, if a resource has a mandatory field (eg. groups `members`), then we cannot do a `PUT` with just the `permissions` values. This is because a PUT can lead to a creation, and the `members` fields has to be provided.

On other resources, which have no mandatory field, it is perfectly possible to only provide `permissions`.
 
But, I believe we should make every resources behave the same way.

For example, when we'll implement the edition of permissions in Kinto-admin, we don't want to have to pass the `data` if it was not changed.

Two solutions:

* Add a default value (`[]`) for the groups members attribute (*my prefered one, trivial and not absurd*)
* Allow `data` to be omitted only when the `PUT` replaces an existing object (*more complex to implement, but would work for any resource with mandatory fields*)

issue_title: bug in waverec of n-dimensional data when axis != 0
issue_body:
The shape check that occurs here:

https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/96ee638e619d81cee316dabc1c81b90383989d8c/pywt/_multilevel.py#L146-L147

is assuming a transform along axis 0.  I missed updating it when I added `axis` support to all DWT routines.

This means that for some combinations of data size and wavelet, mismatched approximation and detail coefficient arrays can occur causing a `RuntimeError` to be raised.

A minimal example of the problem (no error occurs if axis=0):

import numpy as np
import pywt

c = pywt.wavedec(np.ones((16, 16)), 'db2', axis=1)
arr = pywt.waverec(c, 'db2', axis=1)

issue_title: Do URL and path joining with urljoin
issue_body:
Many places in the tests combine URLs and paths with simple string addition. This has the potential to produce incorrect results.

Pure addition of path parts is dangerous, because standard "urljoin" logic, which follows rules in various RFCs, behaves differently. For example:

```
>>> '/a/b/c' + '/d/e'
'/a/b/c/d/e'
>>> urljoin('/a/b/c', '/d/e')
'/d/e'
```

Pulp should produce paths that behave correctly with urljoin, and we've had problems in the past with developers making "relative paths" that have a leading slash.

Relative paths with leading slashes are just one demonstration of how the behavior is different. Here is another where string addition would produce an incorrect result:

```
>>> urljoin('http://foo.com', 'a/b/c')
'http://foo.com/a/b/c'
```

Example of the undesirable use of string addition: https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/668f4b7fd5e88c5f9290e4d3b251be587e026f43/pulp_smash/utils.py#L106

I expect most clients of our REST API will use a urljoin implementation to combine paths, and pulp-smash should do the same.

issue_title: Split tests into sections
issue_body:
All tests currently reside in `pulp_smash/tests/`. This works, but it doesn't scale well. How can a user find the kind of test they're looking for?

I'd recommend changing the layout from this:

```
pulp_smash
├── config
│   └── …
└── tests
    ├── test_content_applicability
    └── test_login
```

To this:

```
pulp_smash
├── config
│   └── …
└── tests
    ├── api_v1
    │   └── …
    ├── api_v2
    │   ├── test_content_applicability
    │   └── test_login
    └── cli
        └── …
```

issue_title: Tutorial on relay error
issue_body:
I was doing the tutorial and found this error on the page with the custom filters  filters http://docs.graphene-python.org/projects/django/en/latest/filtering/#custom-filtersets


The example has:
```
class AnimalFilter(django_filters.FilterSet):
    # Do case-insensitive lookups on 'name'
    name = django_filters.CharFilter(lookup_type='iexact')

    class Meta:
        model = Animal
        fields = ['name', 'genus', 'is_domesticated']

    @property
    def qs(self):
        # The query context can be found in self.request.
        return super(AnimalFilter, self).qs.filter(owner=self.request.user)
```

The ` django_filters.CharFilter(lookup_type='iexact')` will give an `TypeError:__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'lookup_type'` it should be changed to:

`name = django_filters.CharFilter(lookup_expr=['iexact'])`

Second thing is the function qs  is not really clear on how you get `self.request.user` it mentions "set in context.user" that is not really clear what that is.

issue_title: AuthorizationView throws 502
issue_body:
First, let me say thanks for making such an amazing package!

I am running into an issue whereby users on our production server request a token using the url `https://api.meural.com/oauth/authorize/?client_id=<client_id>&response_type=code&state=<state>&scope=read+write&redirect_uri=<redirect_uri>` and the server throws a 502. If the user then clears cookies, the problem goes away. Our urls are as follows

```
from django.conf.urls import url
import oauth2_provider.views as oauth2_views

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^authorize/?$', oauth2_views.AuthorizationView.as_view(), name="authorize"),
    url(r'^token/?$', oauth2_views.TokenView.as_view(), name="token"),
    url(r'^revoke-token/?$', oauth2_views.RevokeTokenView.as_view(), name="revoke-token")
]
```

We have regular Token based authentication on the site in addition to oAuth Token based authentication. At first I thought that may be causing some conflict, but I'm having trouble reproducing the issue reliably based on that hypothesis. 

Am digging into the code now. I see `validate_authorization_request` inside the `get` method:

```
        try:
            scopes, credentials = self.validate_authorization_request(request)
        except OAuthToolkitError as error:
            # Application is not available at this time.
            return self.error_response(error, application=None)

```

Could `error_response` actually trigger a `502`? Any other ideas about why this may be happening. Thanks in advance for your help!

issue_title: calibrated dnn model with monotonicity
issue_body:
unfortunately only example code that uses calibrated dnn model doesn't have any monotonicity constraint defined for any of the features. Therefore the code ignores the _projections_ that is returned by `input_calibration_layer_from_hparams` [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/lattice/blob/master/examples/uci_census.py#L419). I struggle to figure out how to use the projections ops in a scenario where the modeled function is supposed to be partially monotonic at least with regards to one feature. The only relevant documentation that can help me out was the comment on the projection ops [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/lattice/blob/498d01f1ef81958d408b265ba0748a9309a9663b/tensorflow_lattice/python/estimators/calibrated.py#L186). However, I am quite puzzled by what is meant by 

> that must be applied at each step (or every so many steps)

I would be glad if someone could clarify how the projection ops should be applied within the net to ensure partial monotonicity.

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: [code-style] change from astyle to clang-format
issue_body:
The OpenThread project currently uses [astyle](http://astyle.sourceforge.net/) to automatically format code and enforce the [OpenThread style guide](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/STYLE_GUIDE.md).  In general, I've found astyle to be fairly limited.

This is a proposal to change to [clang-format 5](http://releases.llvm.org/5.0.0/tools/clang/docs/ClangFormat.html), which provides a number of useful [options](http://releases.llvm.org/5.0.0/tools/clang/docs/ClangFormatStyleOptions.html) not supported by astyle.

Some examples:
- Reordering include statements.
- Reformatting constructor initializers.
- Aligned declarations.
- Aligned end-of-line comments.

This issue supersedes #1466.

issue_title: common data models for getter methods should be improved
issue_body:
Most get_XXX() seem to be relevant for LAN switches/routers only. It is needed to improve the data models to cover better with Carrier PE Routers, BNGs and Firewalls.

Some examples:
get_arp_table() does not specify the routing context. Does it mean the function is for base router instance only? In general it would be meaningful, the get_arp_table() method would have a parameter to define the context such as VRF, IP-VPN, Out-of-band Mgmt or Base router.

get_interfaces() seems to be about physical interfaces, like ports. What about logical interfaces, such as linkagg, ip-interfaces, L2VPN bindings, internal loopbacks, ...?

get_mac_address_table() assumes that the VLAN-Id defines the bridge instance per device. In the SP market typically the VLAN-Id is just port significant - so you could have much more than 4094 ELAN/VPLS services per device.

issue_title: atrous_conv_2d failing if rate != 1
issue_body:
Hi,

there seems to be a bug in the atrous_conv_2d if the rate is not 1. The rate gets converted to a list with 4 entries by utils.autoformat_kernel_2d (https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/layers/conv.py#L338).  This leads to a "unsupported operand type" error when the tensorflow codes tries to substract 1: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/ops/nn_ops.py#L976

This error only occurs on rates != 1 because the tf code checks if the rate is one and finishes before reaching the critical snippet: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/ops/nn_ops.py#L954

issue_title: join 'em
issue_body:
If you can't beat 'em, join 'em. This ticket started (per the original description below) by thinking that we'd kill the Aspen project/brand and reemerge as Simplates, a plugin for Django and Flask. We took a step [back](https://github.com/gratipay/aspen.py/issues/526#issuecomment-172959647) from the brink when we realized that Aspen is *dispatch plus* simplates, and it's worth keeping Aspen for the amalgamation. The basic idea is the same, though: Aspen is becoming a "parasitic web framework," a plugin for Django and Flask.

-----------------

Okay! Wow! Over on the [November 2015 call](https://github.com/gratipay/aspen.py/issues/525), @pjz  and I began talking about the Python 3 port (#524), which led back to the discussion about switching to Werkzeug (see #222 and mention on #357), and then expanded even further: What is the essence of Aspen? Answer: dispatch and simplates. What if we cut out everything non-essential? What if we killed Aspen as a brand and instead folded the dispatcher into [Simplates](), offering shims for Django and Flask?

Django and Flask are the Python web frameworks of record, with Pyramid/Pylons leading the also-rans:

| Framework | PyPI Downloads | PyPI Rank |
|:----------------|----------------:|---------:|
| [Django](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Django) | 16,128,764  | 26  |
| [Flask](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Flask) | 10,290,820  | 45  |
| [Pyramid]() + [Pylons](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Pylons) | 2,055,805<sup>†</sup> | 212<sup>†</sup> |
| [Bottle](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Bottle) | 1,513,060  | 260  |
| [Aspen](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Aspen) | 918,591  | 362  |
| [Zope2](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/Zope2) + [zope.publisher](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/zope.publisher) | 884,100<sup>†</sup> | 368<sup>†</sup> |
| [CherryPy](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/CherryPy) | 749,732  | 410  |
| [web.py](http://pypi-ranking.info/module/web.py) | 437,894  | 604  |

<sup>†</sup> *combined*

The proposal on this ticket is to throw in the towel. Give up. Call it a day. Admit defeat. Stop writing a web framework.

Instead, we would take the most interesting parts of what we've built—simplates and the filesystem dispatcher—and repackage them under the [Simplates](http://simplates.org/) brand. Simplates and dispatch *are* interesting and unique. We've already got a [shim for Django](https://gist.github.com/whit537/1ccf19dc5bd95363564d). We should be able to adapt that for Flask without too much trouble, so the `simplates` library would look something like this:

```
simplates
simplates.dispatch
simplates.renderers
simplates.resources
simplates.shims.django
simplates.shims.flask
```

This would allow us to invest our time and effort where we're really adding value, and collaborate with others where we're currently reinventing the wheel.

issue_title: RENN stopping criterion
issue_body:
There is an issue with RENN. The algorithm does not stop repeating when the imbalancing is already corrected leading to potential unique class data sampling.

For instance, taking:

```python
import numpy as np
from imblearn.under_sampling import  RepeatedEditedNearestNeighbours()

X = np.array([[ 2.59928271,  0.93323465],
              [ 0.25738379,  0.95564169],
              [ 1.42772181,  0.526027  ],
              [ 1.92365863,  0.82718767],
              [-0.10903849, -0.12085181],
              [-0.284881  , -0.62730973],
              [ 0.57062627,  1.19528323],
              [ 0.03394306,  0.03986753],
              [ 0.78318102,  2.59153329],
              [ 0.35831463,  1.33483198],
              [-0.14313184, -1.0412815 ],
              [ 0.01936241,  0.17799828],
              [-1.25020462, -0.40402054],
              [-0.09816301, -0.74662486],
              [-0.01252787,  0.34102657],
              [ 0.52726792, -0.38735648],
              [ 0.2821046 , -0.07862747],
              [ 0.05230552,  0.09043907],
              [ 0.15198585,  0.12512646],
              [ 0.70524765,  0.39816382]])
Y = np.array([1, 2, 1, 1, 0, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 0, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 2, 1])

renn = RepeatedEditedNearestNeighbours()

x, y = renn.fit_sample(X, Y)
```

This will lead to: 

```
.../imblearn/base.py:93: RuntimeWarning: Only one class detected, something will get wrong
  RuntimeWarning)
```

Since that after some iteration there is only one class.
The stopping criterion need to be change such that we stop before that.

issue_title: gethostname not defined error
issue_body:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 16, in <module>
    test = LogDNAHandler(key, options)
  File "/home/vinod/work/venv_python3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/logdna/logdna.py", line 18, in __init__
    self.hostname = options['hostname'] if 'hostname' in options else gethostname()
NameError: name 'gethostname' is not defined
```
This is because `gethostname` is not imported. Easy fix is to call `socket.gethostname()`.

`self.hostname = options['hostname'] if 'hostname' in options else gethostname()`
should be replaced with
`self.hostname = options['hostname'] if 'hostname' in options else socket.gethostname()`
  
logdna version is 1.2.3

issue_title: Pybind11 causes clang 3.3 and clang 3.4 to segfault
Guidelines for packaging projects using pybind11
issue_body:
Hi,
I am greatly enjoying using pybind11. I am not sure if this question goes beyond the scope of this project, but I am sure I am not the only person dealing with this issue. 

I have a mixed C++ / Python project, mainly a C++ library, a wrapper for this library using pybind11, some additional library functionality in pure python, using the pybind11 module, and lots of tests and usage samples in both C++ and Python.

So now, how can I properly package my library / SDK? The C++ part is easy, use CMake to build, and install/package. But how about my pure python files? 
Do I call [Python from CMake](https://github.com/pybind/python_example) or rather [CMake from Python](https://github.com/pybind/python_example), or is it possible to stay in CMake entirely?
The second link (it's the example linked in the documentation) unfortunately only builds the extension, but unfortunately does not distribute any other Python files with it.

How about my users? How would they like to install my module? 
On Ubuntu, standard way would be to install a .deb package for my C++ SDK, and then use pip or setup.py for the python part. On Windows? SDK installer and then build the extensions for a specific Python version? Deliver prebuild extensions and register them with existing Python installations?

As I said, this might be (and probably is) beyond the scope of this project, but some additional pointers in the documentation would be very helpful.

issue_title: Standalone mode does not bind to ipv6
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 276624626, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20manual%20authenticator', 'name': 'area: manual authenticator', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 418985762, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20standalone', 'name': 'area: standalone', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954789, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '00ccff', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the letsencrypt client does not bind to ipv6 in standalone mode nor do the commands given in manual mode. While this may not seem too significant, but for people with ipv6 only machines (like me) this is quite a big problem. My suggestion would be to somehow bind to both ipv4 and ipv6 at the same time.

Either way, what you're doing is a great service to the global internet community, keep on rocking!

issue_title: Perfect Score on SSL Labs Test. Possible as of 2016?
issue_body:
Hi guys. I've been trying hard to get a perfect score with Nginx + LetsEncrypt SSL cert on the SSL Labs Server Test. I'm getting A+, no problem. Cert is only incompatible with IE6/XP and Java 6 which is more than fine. Those are built on ancient technology and should not be used anymore.

The main thing is: I simply can't get a 100% score at all. I've followed multiple tutorials claiming to be able to provide me with a perfect score, which doesn't happen.

The most I can get out of the test is:

90% - Cipher Strength

Is it possible to get perfect score with Nginx using Lets Encrypt certs today?

issue_title: `letsencrypt renew` doesn't update outdated symlinks in live directory
issue_body:
I had a case reported here:
https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/renew-not-working-for-expired-certificate/17883

`letsencrypt renew` managed to get & write new certificates to `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/` but didn't update the symlinks in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/` (probably I didn't run the command as root -- my error).

Subsequent runs of `letsencrypt renew` told me my certificate was updated but the `live` symlink didn't change from '1' to '2' files so I thought the renewal wasn't working properly.

I think `letsencrypt renew` should try to defend against errors like this by checking the `live` symlinks point to the latest version of the `archive` files, even when it doesn't request a renewal.

issue_title: On Restart: Query all events from the latest know block
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

When Raiden restarts it needs to catch up with the events that happened while the node was offline.

Currently a Raiden node must be synchronized with 3 smart contracts `Registry`, `ChannelManagerContract`, and `NettingChannelContract`, these are the details about the data store in these contracts:

Smart Contract | Details
--- | ---
Registry | Stores the list of registered token networks, since the only available action is to *add* a new ChannelManager, we are safe with loading *only* the latest data from the most recent block on restart.
ChannelManagerContract | Store a list of all opened channels, this data is used for path finding *and* to know the channels the node is participating. The `ChannelDeleted` event for channel the node is a participant is required to do cleanup
NettingChannelContract | The state of a netting channel is only changed through the handling of block chain events, so the netting channel events are required to properly handle the channel settlement.

## Solution

Get the latest Block state change and query for all events since then.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Restore the latest know block from the `Block` state change.
  - [ ] Add a map from the state change *class* object to an identifier (allows for code to be moved around and to check for unique identifiers easily).
  - [ ] Add a indexed column on the state_change table to store the state change identifier.
  - [ ] On startup query the database for the latest state change of type Block to restore the latest block number.
- [ ] Query the ethereum node for relevant events since the latest know block

issue_title: Raiden need's to tell the user if there is a bug
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

In case of a dangerous bug, the Raiden client should tell the user to leave the token network, and wait for a client update. This issue is related to #1810 

## Solution

We should have a repository or a hosted file, which the client can query using a background worker, if that contains some predefined text, that the client app should warn the user.

issue_title: Address Healthcheck Synchronization
issue_body:
Suggestion by @hackaugusto:

Instead of adding a new task, we could add the ping logic in the _send_queued_message:

    Send ping to check if partner is online
        if ping unconfirmed -> node is unavailable
            wait for partner to come online (if any message is received from partner)
    loop:
        if there is a message in the queue
            until message confirmed, have retries, or partner is back online
                send message
                wait for confirmation or timeout
                if max_retries == 0 node is unavailable (the queue message worked and the Ping)
                    wait for partner to come back online (available if any message is received from partner)
                    restart max_retries counter
                elseif unconfirmed, decrement max_retries
        if no message in the queue and send-ping-time passed
            send Ping and wait for confirmation
            if ping unconfirmed -> node is unavailable
                wait for partner to come back online (available if any message is received from partner)

This would make one task per channel, we eliminate synchronizations issues for messages that have max_retries, and we can start these queues in a randomization loop as suggested by @konradkonrad

@hackaugusto  @konradkonrad  let's take the discussion here.

issue_title: Fix Decoding in NettingChannelLibrary.sol
issue_body: Decoding in NettingChannelLibrary.sol is probably not working as expected. Arrays and other variable length types in memory in solidity have an `int256` containing its length before anything else. So essentially everywhere in the library where [decoding happens](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L442) we need to check 32 bytes further.

issue_body:
Right now after the SoS Jupyter frontend starts, it sends an request to the kernel for a list of kernels using code

```
events.on('kernel_ready.Kernel', request_kernel_list); 
```

The kernel then compiles a list of available kernels, basically running `jupyter kernelspec list` and send the frontend the list. However, this has caused `TravisCI` to [fail](https://travis-ci.org/vatlab/SOS/builds/220428500), and [removing this function](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/commit/585610add180cbfa90b6bc07089c42b8697835f6) has [fixed the issue](https://travis-ci.org/vatlab/SOS/builds/220530360).

The question is then how to send the kernel list information efficiently to the frontend.

issue_title: %skip magic is still needed
issue_body:
I removed the `%skip` magic (#401) because it is extremely difficult to work correctly if we would like to keep the original output of the cell. However, a `%skip` magic is still needed because steps of workflows defined in a Jupyter notebook sometimes cannot be executed (e.g. auxiliary steps) so the workflow would not be able to execute completely if it contains such cells.

A `%skip` magic would allow Jupyter to skip these cells, although the cell can still be used as part of workflows because the workflow extraction process skips magics.

issue_title: Return path from path + str, allow path + path.
issue_body:
`path.py` treats `path` as string so:

```
str = path + str
str = str + path 
```

existing behavior:
```
str = path + str
```
There is no `str + path`, and `str += path` would fail. The tests work because they happen to have the form of `str += path + str`.

Proposed change:
```
path = path + str
```
which is similar to

```
path = path / str
```
and would allow

```
path + str + str + path
```

issue_title: MNIST target_transform
issue_body:
In the MNIST dataset the target is a label (not a PIL image). Hence trying to do a `target_transform` wouldn't really work right?

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/13d1a6cc955ca9a49f240a99b44ddcc8a685efbc/torchvision/datasets/mnist.py#L67-L82

Shouldn't we get rid of the `target_transform` input?
Alternatively we could do the following if there is a `target_transform`:

```
    def __getitem__(self, index):
        """
        Args:
            index (int): Index
        Returns:
            tuple: (image, target) where target is index of the target class.
        """
        if self.train:
            img, target = self.train_data[index], self.train_labels[index]
        else:
            img, target = self.test_data[index], self.test_labels[index]

        # doing this so that it is consistent with all other datasets
        # to return a PIL Image
        image = Image.fromarray(img.numpy(), mode='L')

        if self.transform is not None:
            img = self.transform(image)

        if self.target_transform is not None:
            target = self.target_transform(image)

        return img, target
```

issue_title: Load Image From like caffe's LMDB list
issue_body:
I suggest to add a IO for read images from a list like this to support custom image data input,

```
img1 label1
img2 label2
...
```

which like caffe's LMDB list format.

I implement it by referencing `torchvision/datasets/folder.py`

```python
import torch.utils.data as data

from PIL import Image
import os
import os.path

def default_loader(path):
	return Image.open(path).convert('RGB')

def default_flist_reader(flist):
	"""
	flist format: impath label\nimpath label\n ...(same to caffe's filelist)
	"""
	imlist = []
	with open(flist, 'r') as rf:
		for line in rf.readlines():
			impath, imlabel = line.strip().split()
			imlist.append( (impath, int(imlabel)) )
					
	return imlist

class ImageFilelist(data.Dataset):
	def __init__(self, root, flist, transform=None, target_transform=None,
			flist_reader=default_flist_reader, loader=default_loader):
		self.root   = root
		self.imlist = flist_reader(flist)		
		self.transform = transform
		self.target_transform = target_transform
		self.loader = loader

	def __getitem__(self, index):
		impath, target = self.imlist[index]
		img = self.loader(os.path.join(self.root,impath))
		if self.transform is not None:
			img = self.transform(img)
		if self.target_transform is not None:
			target = self.target_transform(target)
		
		return img, target

	def __len__(self):
		return len(self.imlist)
```

The usage is same to `ImageFolder` class,

```python
 46     train_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(
 47         ImageFilelist(root="../place365_challenge/data_256/", flist="../place365_challenge/places365_train_challenge.txt",
 48             transform=transforms.Compose([transforms.RandomSizedCrop(224),

 51         ])),

demo code: https://gist.github.com/matthew-z/3d5e326fa8aa8b147a0e9429b21f8cb6

I started a small cluster without autoscaler: 1 header and 1 worker node. Both of them have 8 CPU cores. I installed latest ray from https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/ray-wheels/latest/ray-0.5.0-cp36-cp36m-manylinux1_x86_64.whl

issue_title: Serialization of numpy.int64 is not handled properly.
issue_body:
This bug can be reproduced by doing the following.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init(start_ray_local=True, num_workers=1)

@ray.remote
def f(x):
  print([x])
  return x

ray.get(f.remote(np.int64(3)))  # This prints ['\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'] and returns '\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'.
```

Note that the following code works.

```python
ray.get(ray.put(np.int64(3)))  # prints 3
```

The same problem occurs with other numpy data types.

issue_title: Add ability to parallel copy with per-host remote & local file paths
issue_body:
### Motivation
I'd like the the ability to specify per-host remote and local file paths for `ParallelSSH2Client`'s `copy_remote_file` function (or possibly as a separate function). My specific use-cases:

#### Per-host remote paths
I have a Linux script which generates a file with a randomized path (specifically, using `mktemp`) and outputs the path on stdout. 

Right now, it's easy enough for me to run said script in parallel across my list of hosts and capture the output paths from each, but I can't easily copy each file produced back to my local host in parallel.

#### Per-host local paths
I have a set of shared files I need to retrieve from a list of hosts, but the local paths I want to store them in differ non-trivially per-host (the list of hosts is logically divided into various groups and sub-groups, and I want to store the files into a somewhat complicated directory tree reflecting this).

Obviously, I can always just retrieve the files to a single location with the current code and move them from there to their final local paths, but this presents at least two downsides:
1. I'd essentially have to replicate existing work to check for files which failed to copy + handle the file moves in parallel, making the code much less clean than if parallel-ssh were simply copying them to the correct path to begin with.
2. While not an issue for me right now, if I were using this to move large files around I could potentially fill up the fs for my single local directory. Whereas, directly copying to multiple local paths would allow me to spread the space requirement across several mounts instead of needing a single mount with enough space to temporarily hold all the files.

------

### Implementation
I've taken a look at the existing code and I'd be happy enough to implement this functionality myself, but I'm not sure what kind of interface would be preferred for it. I can see two obvious approaches:

1. Add a `host_paths` optional argument to `copy_remote_function`, taking something similar to `host_config` (see below), with un-specified hosts/paths in it defaulting to the normal `remote_file` and `local_file` argument behaviour.
```
host_paths = {
    'host1': {'remote_file': 'a_file',
              'local_file':  'host1/a_file',
              'recurse':      True},
    'host2': {'remote_file': 'b_file'},
    'host3': {'local_file':  '/another/dir/host3_file'},
}
```
2. Add a separate function entirely to handle parallel per-host copies, and either directly take something like the above `host_paths` structure as an argument (either ignoring or (optionally?) erroring for un-specified hosts), or possibly take a callback function run per-host to generate the paths/options

issue_title: Populate_descendants does not work for part models
issue_body:
- in part.py line 709:
descendants_flat_list = list(self._client.parts(pk=self.id, descendants='children', batch=batch))'
- should look like this:
descendants_flat_list = list(self._client.parts(pk=self.id, category=self.category, descendants='children', batch=batch))'

Cause .parts() has category set as instance by default. So using model.populate_descendants() will find no descendants because it will only check for instances.

issue_title: Bug with URL quote/unquote
issue_body:
in caldav/objects.py:
DAVObject._handle_prop_response() unqotes key it returns:
line 174:            
       href = unquote(r.find('.//' + dav.Href.tag).text)
but in DAVObject.get_properties it is compared to URL path, which is quoted:  
lines 209-218: 
        path = self.url.path
        exchange_path = self.url.path + '/'

        if path in list(properties.keys()):
            rc = properties[path]
        elif exchange_path in list(properties.keys()):
            rc = properties[exchange_path]
        else:
            raise Exception("The CalDAV server you are using has "
                            "a problem with path handling.")
So, if base path contains quoted characters, they do not match and the server is blamed.
Following modification makes it working for me:
lines 209-210: 
        path = unquote(self.url.path)
        exchange_path = path + '/'

Also, a small optimisation in lines 212 and 214:
        'in properties' is equivalent of 'in list(properties.keys())', but more effective.

issue_title: ParkingLot and Queue are embarrassingly slow
issue_body:
@sorcio sent a small benchmark that I don't think I can share publically, but that implements a simple message broker: clients can connect and say "send this blob of data to channel X" or "please send me the data from channel X", and it brokers between them. His initial version used a `trio.Queue` for message passing and a `trio.hazmat.ParkingLot` to implement a little `Event`-like object. It turned out that some ridiculous proportion of runtime was going into these, like when I dropped in a some simple non-fair versions using a `set` to track sleeping tasks, the overall execution got 2x faster.

Looking at `ParkingLot`, I think at least for now we should be able to make it much faster without losing any features, by replacing the `SortedDict`-of-tickets with an `OrderedDict`-of-tasks. The trick to getting rid of the tickets is that when we requeue, we can go modify `task._abort_fn` directly. (Hey, we're in `_core`, might as well make use of it!) This means that requeuing tasks from one lot to another would put them behind the tasks that were waiting there rather than preserving the global order, but I think that's fine, maybe even better than the current system. This also means if we *wanted* to add tags to sleeping tasks to implement task-fair RWLocks, we could do that for "free", by storing it in the value entry in the `OrderedDict` (otherwise the value would just be `None`).

I'm not sure if switching to a shared global ParkingLot API would be better or not. It would allow us to skip allocating a `ParkingLot` entirely for uncontended objects – like a `Queue(1)` that just gets used once wouldn't need a put lot at all. OTOH with a global object we'd have to jump through several dict lookups each time to find it. It likely doesn't make a big difference either way, but it's probably worth benchmarking at some point.

I'm not sure if the Queue issue is the same or different – Queue obviously depends on ParkingLot, but has a somewhat overcomplicated implementation on top of that using a bunch of semaphores and stuff.

We could redo Queue as:
* `put`: if there's a `get` waiting, hand it the object; otherwise, immediately append to deque, and if over capacity, go to sleep.
* `get`: if there's data in the queue, take it and wake a `put`; otherwise, go to sleep and wait to be handed some data.

Downside: this requires `ParkingLot` grow back the ability to hand off an object to the woken task. I think this is OK? Upside: in this model, `Queue(0)` automatically works correctly. Neutral: this actually give even stricter FIFO fairness than we have currently -- right now if G1 and G2 block in `get`, and then P1 and P2 call `put` in quick succession so that G1 and G2 are both woken up on the same tick, then it's possible for G1 to get P2's object and G2 to get P1's. This is only possible when G1 and G2 wake up on the same tick though, so I don't know that it matters – what we have now is not strict FIFO, but that's not the same as fair and it'd be hard to say that what we have isn't fair. Actually, if we're OK with this kind of fairness, then maybe we can drop the requirement of handing off data through `ParkingLot.unpark`?

Maybe drop the whole `join` API too because ugh, it's this second specialized synchronization primitive bolted onto `Queue` that's rarely useful.

issue_title: Check CapacityLimiter's handling of _pending_borrowers
issue_body:
I was just reading the code for an unrelated reason, and realized that this code looks very suspicious:

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/65729b121c55ff0aa1f5d43bd9cdcd6a81e54f0c/trio/_sync.py#L286-L296

Specifically, if the call to `await self._lot.park()` gets cancelled, it looks like we don't clear `self._pending_borrowers[task]`, which could be a memory leak (since it pins task objects in memory).

issue_title: Verify scope for async fixtures
issue_body:
Only async fixture with default `function` scope can work with trio.
Currently, an async fixture with another scope (i.e. `module`, `class` or `session`) will be considered as a regular function and end up as a coroutine in the test function.

This is most likely an inconsistent behavior. A much better solution would be to check the scope of the async fixture before test and raise an error if it's not a correct one.

Following discussion in #17, test file `test_fixture_scope.py` has been removed until this feature is added to avoid core dump in Python 3.5

```python
import pytest


@pytest.mark.parametrize('scope', ['class', 'module', 'session'])
def test_not_allowed_scopes(testdir, scope):

    testdir.makepyfile(
        """
        import pytest

        @pytest.fixture(scope=%r)
        async def fix1():
            return 'fix1'

        @pytest.mark.trio
        async def test_base(fix1):
            pass  # Crash should have occures before arriving here
    """ % scope
    )

    result = testdir.runpytest()

    result.assert_outcomes(error=1)


@pytest.mark.parametrize('scope', ['function'])
def test_allowed_scopes(testdir, scope):

    testdir.makepyfile(
        """
        import pytest

        @pytest.fixture(scope=%r)
        async def fix1():
            return 'fix1'

        @pytest.mark.trio
        async def test_base(fix1):
            assert fix1 == 'fix1'
    """ % scope
    )

    result = testdir.runpytest()

    result.assert_outcomes(passed=1)
```

issue_title: Implement monitoring via Elasticsearch + Kibana
issue_body:
Currently, the only information we provide the user about the progress of their workflow is via logging. We would like to extend the information collected, and present this information in a more useful way. The goal of the monitoring system should be to provide an easy-to-understand overview of the current state of the workflow, and to help the user spot problems for faster debugging.

The approach we're proposing is to send information to an Elasticsearch database, and to build a kibana dashboard for viewing it. Sending the information to the database will happen in two places: in the main parsl process, and remotely, where the task is running. @yadudoc has started a sketch [here](https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/pull/322) that sends some simple information to the database from the main parsl process side, and includes a link to the Elasticsearch instance he's running, which you should use for testing. We should all brainstorm what kind of information will be most useful and collect it here, but to start out, very basic information is sufficient, for example: a table of the task ids and statuses; a plot of time vs number of tasks finished, hostnames tasks are running on, task runtime duration, etc. We do not currently have anything implemented for the remote side. The rough idea would be that you would implement some kind of wrapper, which would run around the main task, and send information back to the database. [Here](https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/blob/master/parsl/app/app.py#L60) is an example of a simple wrapper, which you would, for example, hook up by replacing `func` on line 19 of the same file with `app_wrapper(func)`. That wrapper could use a similar approach as what Yadu implemented to send information back to the database. At first, stuff like the task run time, hostname, etc, would be good. Eventually we'd want to add resource monitoring information like CPU usage, memory usage, disk usage, etc. This you could implement yourself using (probably) stuff like [this](https://docs.python.org/3/library/resource.html), or maybe it would be worth checking out what's out there in terms of ready-made tools (a quick search brought up [this](https://github.com/nicolargo/glances), which looks interesting and has a built-in dump-to-elasticsearch.)

One thing we haven't thought out very well, but that needs to be addressed, is some sane way of differentiating between runs, and probably of resetting the database if you want to clear out old monitoring information.

repository_raw_blob returns 404
issue_body:
I'm having trouble getting file contents through api v4 now, and I think it's a problem with the url that the library is hitting.

https://github.com/python-gitlab/python-gitlab/blob/master/gitlab/v4/objects.py#L1878

`url = "/projects/%s/repository/raw_blobs/%s" % (self.id, sha)`

This doesn't seem to match the [Gitlab API specifications](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/repositories.html#raw-blob-content):

>`GET /projects/:id/repository/blobs/:sha/raw`

Sure enough, when I try to hit the endpoint in postman only the latter route returns file contents, whereas `GET /projects/:id/repository/raw_blobs/:sha` consistently returns a 404.

issue_title: broadcasting semantics differ for Matmul and (Add, Max, Min, etc.) ?
issue_body:
The basic binary operators (Add, Max, Min, etc.) allow for the second tensor to be broadcast to the shape of the first tensor, the first tensor is never broadcast. 

According to the Matmul operator documentation, Matlum allows for the more general numpy-like broadcasting:
> If either argument is N-D, N > 2, it is treated as a stack of matrices residing in the last two indexes and broadcast accordingly
 
Moreover, matmul does not require an explicit broadcast flag. I'd prefer broadcasting semantics to be the same for all Operators. 

I have read issue https://github.com/onnx/onnx/issues/83, how close are we to coming to an agreement on how broadcasting will work?

sqlalchemy.exc.InternalError: (pymysql.err.InternalError) (1305, 'FUNCTION flask_test.strftime does not exist') [SQL: 'SELECT strftime(%(strftime_1)s, `functionCalls`.time) AS `newTime`, count(`functionCalls`.execution_time) AS count \nFROM `functionCalls` \nWHERE `functionCalls`.time >= %(time_1)s AND `functionCalls`.time <= %(time_2)s GROUP BY `newTime`'] [parameters: {'strftime_1': '%Y-%m-%d %H:00:00', 'time_2': datetime.datetime(2018, 5, 3, 23, 59, 59), 'time_1': datetime.datetime(2018, 4, 13, 0, 0)}] (Background on this error at: http://sqlalche.me/e/2j85)
```
The problem doesn't appear with SQLite, so we're using some db-specific code, probably `strftime`.

issue_title: Poor results from a Drop-Seq bam file with "velocyto run"
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for Velocyto, it's very interesting.

I try to launch "velocyto run" on our data.
Our scRNA-seq protocol is inspired by the STRT-Seq protocol. The results are in UMI counts and the reads are from the 5 prime end of the mRNA (not like 10x where there are from the 3 prime end).
I used the Drop-Seq pipeline (and STAR) to obtain a mapped bam file where the cell barcode and UMI barcode are contained in the XC and XM tags.
I noticed that counter.py takes care of these specific tags from Drop-Seq.

Unfortunately, I have got very poor results when I launch "Velocyto run" on my bam which contains 96 cells. Only a few genes are detected and we do not have a lot of spliced, unspliced and ambiguous values.

Also, it may be part of this issue: In R, when I look at the rownames(ldat$spliced), there are a lot of duplicated genes... 

Did I miss something?
Normally (without velocyto), I have more than 1000 genes detected per cell.

Thank you,
Inaki Cervera-Marzal

issue_title: command help should be shown if parameters are missing
issue_body:
Running `nrfutil.py` standalone will give me some help:

```
$ python nrfutil.py 
Usage:  [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:
  -v, --verbose            Show verbose information.
  -o, --output <filename>  Log output to file
(etc)
```

Same happens when I'm missing a command:

```
$ python nrfutil.py dfu
Usage: dfu [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  This set of commands supports Device Firmware Upgrade procedures over both
  BLE and serial transports.

Options:
  --help  Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  ble         Update the firmware on a device over a BLE connection.
(etc)
```

Note that the output of `nrfutil dfu` is the same as `nrfutil dfu --help`

**However**, when missing a required parameter, the output is different:

```
$ python nrfutil.py dfu serial
Usage: dfu serial [OPTIONS]

Error: Missing option "-pkg" / "--package".
```

That's not really useful.

Compare with the output of `nrfutil.py dfu serial --help`, which is what I would expect. Note how information about required fields is there to help me.

```
$ python nrfutil.py dfu serial --help
Usage: dfu serial [OPTIONS]

  Perform a Device Firmware Update on a device with a bootloader that
  supports serial DFU.

Options:
  -pkg, --package PATH            Filename of the DFU package.  [required]
(etc)
```

issue_title: Community Lacks A Code of Merit
issue_body:
Per the email thread where I took a PHD to task for not using the scientific method and being intellectually lazy, this community lacks a set of community contribution merit standards and as a result people who wish to contribute have no official guide on what merits their work have.

I have personally worked on projects without a merit standard , as well as ones where they explicitly list out merits. 

Based on the feedback form that email thread, I suspect that we should put more effort into ours.  I would prefer personally we simply start with http://code-of-merit.org/ as a base and edit as needed, but I wanted to open the floor to the rest of the team.

issue_title: Allow reading the status code
issue_body:
How to read status code? According to documentation only options are to check if response was successful or compare it with a value. When trying to print out the status code, only object representation is shown. Consider following:

```python
>>> browser.visit('/foobar')
>>> browser.status_code.status_code.is_success()
True
>>> browser.status_code == 200
False
>>> print browser.status_code
<splinter.request_handler.status_code.StatusCode object at 0xa435a6c>
```

Change request: print out the human readable response instead.

issue_title: HWM for SUB socket does NOT DROP data
issue_body:
As I understand, hwm setting for SUB socket will drop new data if it reaches queue limit and drop new data.
However, in my test, the queue size of SUB socket does not drop the new data. It just saves all data into the subscriber's queue.

These are my test codes:
= server =========================
import zmq
import time

context = zmq.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
socket.bind("tcp://*:5556")

count = 0
while True:
    count += 1
    socket.send("%d" % count)
    print count
    time.sleep(0.5)

= client =========================
import zmq
import time

seconds = 2

context = zmq.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
socket.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, "")
socket.setsockopt(zmq.HWM, 2)
socket.connect("tcp://127.0.0.1:5556")

while True:
    count = socket.recv()
    print count
    time.sleep(seconds)

In this test, the subscriber must print jumping numbers because some data are dropped. But it doesn't.
